Concrete trial: thread A (sleep 0.5s, returns 'A_RESULT') and thread B (sleep 0.1s, returns 'B_RESULT') — observed mapping was A→'B_RESULT' and B→'A_RESULT'. Worse failure modes are possible: if both threads write commands to stdin concurrently, the lines can interleave at byte level and the worker's json.loads will fail on garbage, after which the worker emits 'bad command' and is permanently desynced relative to the parent's expectations. The Kernel class makes no claim about thread-safety, but it is also not actually safe and there is no lock.
findings.expected: Either: (a) document that execute is single-threaded only and add a threading.Lock asserting it, or (b) add an internal lock around the stdin-write/stdout-read critical section so concurrent callers serialize.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile, threading
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.kernel import Kernel
from pathlib import Path
k = Kernel(workspace=Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())); k.start()
out = []
def w(n, s):
    o = k.execute(f"import time; time.sleep({s}); '{n}_R'")
    out.append((n, o.result))
t1 = threading.Thread(target=w, args=('A', 0.5)); t2 = threading.Thread(target=w, args=('B', 0.1))
t1.start(); t2.start(); t1.join(); t2.join()
print(out)  # observed cross-mapping in 1/3 trials
k.stop()
findings.suggested_fix: Add `self._lock = threading.Lock()` in __init__ and wrap the stdin-write→stdout-readline section of execute() (and reset()) with `with self._lock:`. The same lock should also exclude calls from stop() which today races with execute().

findings.title: First execute() after a timeout returns a spurious 0.0s 'cell timed out' Observation
findings.observed: After a cell times out, kernel.execute sends SIGTERM but does not wait/reap the worker, does not clear self.proc, and does not drain the stdout pipe. The next execute() call sees proc.poll() != None (worker exited) BUT the body of execute proceeds without restart on the first iteration: `if self.proc is None or self.proc.poll() is not None: self.start()` — wait, this DOES call start() if poll is non-None. Reading the trace: after a 1.0s timeout the followup is `ok=False elapsed=0.00s stderr_head='cell timed out after 0.0s'`. This means start() was called, the new worker received the cmd, but the select-loop saw the previous worker's CLOSED stdout pipe (self.proc is now the NEW Popen so its stdout is fresh — actually the new worker should be fine). The issue: select() returns rlist immediately on a fresh-pipe EOF condition under some conditions, readline returns '', the loop breaks, result is None, code emits the timeout Observation with elapsed≈0. Empirically: 1st followup fails fast, 2nd followup works. So users see one phantom failure per timeout.
findings.expected: After a timeout, the kernel should (a) wait for the SIGTERM'd process to actually exit (proc.wait(2)), (b) drain stderr_buf, (c) on the next execute() spawn a fresh worker cleanly. The first followup execute should succeed, not return a 0.0s phantom timeout.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.kernel import Kernel
from pathlib import Path
k = Kernel(workspace=Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())); k.start()
k.execute('while True: pass', timeout=1.0)
o = k.execute('1+1', timeout=5)
print('1st followup:', o.ok, repr(o.result), o.elapsed_s)  # ok=False elapsed=0.0
print('2nd followup:', k.execute('1+1', timeout=5).result)  # '2'
k.stop()
findings.suggested_fix: On timeout in execute(): SIGTERM, then `try: self.proc.wait(timeout=2) except TimeoutExpired: self.proc.kill(); self.proc.wait()`, then `self.proc = None`. Then the next execute() will go through start() cleanly. Also clear any pending bytes from self.proc.stdout before declaring success on the next call (read until empty with non-blocking flag) — although if proc=None this is moot.

findings.title: Cell that fork()s a child writing to fd 1 leaks lines into the parent kernel's stdout pipe
findings.observed: Cell `pid=os.fork(); ...; os.write(1, b'CHILD_FD1_WRITE\n')` causes the kernel to receive an extra non-prefixed line on its stdout pipe. The select-loop classifies it as 'stray' and prepends it to obs.stdout. Today this is benign (output is folded back), but combined with the marker-forgery finding (#1) it is the same code path: a child can also emit a fake `\x1eFORGE_RESULT\x1e...\n` line and the parser believes it. Worse: a daemon child that survives the parent cell will keep emitting onto fd1 across cells, contaminating later cells' stdout/result.
findings.expected: Either prevent forks (rare for an agent; probably too restrictive), close fd1 in the cell so any child gets a closed pipe (breaks legitimate prints), or accept fork-leak as known and tighten the marker channel (suggested_fix in finding #1).
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile, time
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.kernel import Kernel
from pathlib import Path
k = Kernel(workspace=Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())); k.start()
obs = k.execute("import os, time\npid = os.fork()\nif pid == 0:\n    time.sleep(0.2)\n    os.write(1, b'LATE_FROM_CHILD\\n')\n    os._exit(0)\nelse:\n    'parent'\n")
print('cell1.stdout:', obs.stdout)  # may or may not contain the late write
import time as _t; _t.sleep(0.5)
obs2 = k.execute('1+1')
print('cell2.stdout:', obs2.stdout)  # 'LATE_FROM_CHILD' bleeds in here
print('cell2.result:', obs2.result)  # may be wrong if child wrote a marker
k.stop()
findings.suggested_fix: Tightening the marker-channel (per finding #1) makes this benign. Independently, the worker could install a SIGCHLD handler / track fork() via os.register_at_fork to warn or to refuse fork(). At minimum, document that fork is unsafe and that any persistent child will pollute later cells.

findings.title: Kernel.stop() while a cell is mid-execution leaves the in-flight execute() to time out (2.5s) instead of returning promptly
findings.observed: Calling Kernel.stop() while a long-running execute() is blocked in select() on the same proc.stdout: stop() sends an 'exit' op (worker can't read it because it's busy in the cell), waits 2s, terminates. The blocked execute() in the other thread eventually sees its readline return '' and exits the select-loop with result=None, emitting an Observation('cell timed out after 2.5s ...'). End-to-end this is bounded (~2.5s) and clean (no exception leaks), but the in-flight execute() loses ~1.5s after stop() already returned, which is a poor signal for callers waiting on shutdown. Also: stop() raised a noisy `BrokenPipeError: [Errno 32] Broken pipe` from the `Exception ignored in: <_io.TextIOWrapper>` finalizer because the stdin pipe is closed without flushing.
findings.expected: stop() during an in-flight cell should: (1) immediately wake the blocked reader (close stdout from the parent side, or signal a shutdown event the select-loop checks), (2) cause the blocked execute() to return Observation(ok=False, stderr='kernel stopping'), (3) not emit an 'Exception ignored in TextIOWrapper' finalizer noise.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile, threading, time
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.kernel import Kernel
from pathlib import Path
k = Kernel(workspace=Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())); k.start()
out = {}
def run():
    out['o'] = k.execute('import time\nfor _ in range(50):\n    time.sleep(0.1)\n', timeout=30)
t = threading.Thread(target=run); t.start()

t0 = time.monotonic()

t.join()

findings.suggested_fix: Track an internal shutdown event; have execute() check it inside the select-loop tick. Or, simpler: in stop(), after sending 'exit' and timing out, close self.proc.stdout from the parent side BEFORE calling terminate(); the readline in the other thread immediately returns ''. Also wrap the stdin.write in stop() in `try/except (BrokenPipeError, OSError): pass` AND set self.proc.stdin = None to suppress the finalizer-time BrokenPipeError.

findings.title: 10 MB stdout cell is fine, but Observation.format() truncates at 4000 chars by default — the kernel returns the full payload, so memory is the only ceiling
findings.observed: Cell that writes 10 MB to stdout: ok=True, elapsed=0.06s, len(obs.stdout)=10485761, all bytes intact (10485760 'y' chars + trailing newline). The redirect_stdout(StringIO) buffers the whole thing in memory inside the worker, then JSON-serializes it across the pipe. There is no streaming and no per-call cap. A pathological cell (`'x' * (1<<30)`) would balloon the worker to 2-4 GB before the result even gets serialized, hang the parent on json.loads of a multi-GB line, and likely cause a crash or OOM.
findings.expected: Either cap stdout per cell (e.g. truncate at N MB inside the worker with a clear marker) or stream stdout to the parent as it's produced. For an LLM-driven agent the model can't usefully consume more than ~16 KB anyway, so capping at e.g. 1 MB inside the worker is a reasonable default and matches the existing 4000-char Observation.format truncation philosophy.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')

from pathlib import Path

o = k.execute("import sys; sys.stdout.write('y' * (10*1024*1024)); print()")
print(len(o.stdout))  # 10485761
k.stop()
findings.suggested_fix: In the worker, wrap the StringIO with a max-bytes guard (truncate + append '... [truncated NN MB]'). 1 MB cap is plenty for an agent. This avoids both worker-side OOM and parent-side multi-GB json.loads.

Result: ok=True, result='42' as expected. The fake marker shows up only inside obs.stdout's JSON 'stdout' field. Safe in this path. (But see finding #1: os.write(1,...) is a different story.)

findings.title: undo_last does NOT remove files newly added in the undone commit
findings.observed: After committing a new file then calling undo_last(), the new file remains on disk AND gets re-committed by the follow-up snapshot commit. So a 'forge undo' of an `Add file` cell appears to succeed (returns the undone commit) but leaves the file in place. The docstring on undo_last explicitly claims this case is handled ('Files that were created by the undone commit are now deleted from the index; clean them up') but the implementation does not delete them. Probe E in /tmp/forge_stress/probe2.py shows newfile.txt present both before and after undo.
findings.expected: After undo_last(), files created in the undone commit should be removed from the workspace, restoring the workspace to the previous tree state.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile
from pathlib import Path
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.shadow import ShadowGit
ws = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())
s = ShadowGit(workspace=ws); s.init()
(ws/'existing.txt').write_text('base'); s.commit('base')
(ws/'newfile.txt').write_text('new'); s.commit('add newfile')
s.undo_last()
assert not (ws/'newfile.txt').exists(), 'newfile should be gone after undo'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git read-tree --reset -u <target>` followed by `git clean -fd` (limited to tracked-or-not paths under workspace, while excluding .forge/), or use `git checkout-index -af --prefix=...` against the target tree. A simple correct path: `self._git('reset', '--hard', target.sha)` then `self._git('clean', '-fd')` (with a safety predicate that the workspace path equals the configured workspace and is non-root).

findings.title: reset_to silently succeeds when given a non-existent / invalid sha
findings.observed: `reset_to('deadbeef'*5)` returns without raising. The internal `git checkout <bad_sha> -- .` is invoked with check=False, so its non-zero exit and stderr are dropped. Then commit() runs `add -A` and creates an empty `shadow:reset_to deadbee` commit on top, making the operation indistinguishable from a successful reset to an unchanged-state target. Callers get no signal that nothing was reset.
findings.expected: Either raise an exception (preferred — it's an API contract violation) or return a sentinel/Optional[ShadowCommit] indicating failure. Silently writing a misleading commit message hides the failure from the user.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile
from pathlib import Path
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.shadow import ShadowGit
ws = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())
s = ShadowGit(workspace=ws); s.init()
(ws/'a.txt').write_text('a'); s.commit('c1')
s.reset_to('deadbeef' * 5)  # Returns None silently; no error
print([c.message for c in s.log(5)])  # contains 'shadow:reset_to deadbee'
findings.suggested_fix: Validate the sha first: `self._git('rev-parse', '--verify', f'{sha}^{{commit}}')` with check=True (will raise CalledProcessError on bad sha). Then perform the checkout with check=True so any failure surfaces. Same applies to undo_last when the parent ref is unreachable.

findings.title: Files matched by workspace .gitignore are invisible to the shadow snapshot
findings.observed: When the workspace contains a `.gitignore` listing `secret.txt`, writing `secret.txt` and calling commit() produces a snapshot that does NOT include the file (git's `add -A` honors .gitignore). After undo_last, the previously-written secret.txt remains on disk because the shadow never tracked it. Probe 7 confirms `secret_tracked=false`. The module docstring claims 'shadow ignores the same things the workspace's .gitignore would', so this is documented behavior, but it conflicts with the user-stated invariant 'the shadow should see it (we use add -A)' — `add -A` does not override .gitignore. This means agent-written secrets, logs, dist artifacts, etc. are non-revertable by `forge undo`.
findings.expected: If the shadow's purpose is to checkpoint EVERY filesystem mutation regardless of the user's git config, it must use `git add -Af` (force) so .gitignore'd paths are still tracked. Otherwise document loudly that ignored paths are unsnapshot and cannot be undone.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile
from pathlib import Path
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.shadow import ShadowGit
ws = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())
s = ShadowGit(workspace=ws); s.init()
(ws/'.gitignore').write_text('secret.txt\n*.log\n'); s.commit('ig')
(ws/'secret.txt').write_text('hunter2'); s.commit('write secret')
out = s._git('ls-tree','-r','HEAD',check=False).stdout
assert 'secret.txt' not in out  # untracked
s.undo_last()
assert (ws/'secret.txt').exists()  # still there, undo did nothing for it
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `self._git('add', '-A', check=False)` with `self._git('add', '-A', '-f', check=False)` in both init() and commit(). The .forge/.gitignore that you set as core.excludesfile already excludes .forge/ and pyc junk, so -f is safe. Test must be added for the non-tracked-deletion path too.

findings.title: init() writes core.excludesfile pointing to .forge/.gitignore but never overwrites it on subsequent inits
findings.observed: init() is idempotent via the `(self.git_dir / 'HEAD').exists()` check. If a user (or earlier code path) has populated .forge/.gitignore with custom entries, those are preserved across init calls — confirmed in Probe G. That is good for user customization, but it also means if forge ships a new default .gitignore (e.g., adds `node_modules/`), existing workspaces will never pick up the change. There is no migration path.
findings.expected: Either (a) document that .forge/.gitignore is user-owned after first init, or (b) version the file and merge new defaults on init. Currently the behavior is implicit and surprising.
findings.reproducer:

findings.suggested_fix: Make init's idempotency check separately apply to (a) repo creation and (b) defaults file management, e.g., always (re)write a `.forge/.gitignore.defaults` and have core.excludesfile point at a merged file, or simply document the contract.

findings.title: commit() per-call latency ~40ms; up to 4 git subprocess invocations per commit
findings.observed: 50 sequential commits took 1.99s on this machine (39.8ms/commit). Each commit() shells out to git up to 4x: add -A, commit, rev-parse HEAD, and (when called from undo_last/reset_to) the prior checkout. For a hot agent loop with 1000s of cells this becomes a 40+ second tax. Process startup (~10-15ms each) dominates — the work is trivial. 50MB blob commit took 0.92s, mostly hashing.
findings.expected: <10ms per checkpoint commit on a small workspace. Process spawn is the main overhead.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile, time
from pathlib import Path
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.shadow import ShadowGit
ws = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())
s = ShadowGit(workspace=ws); s.init()
t = time.time()
for i in range(50):
    (ws/f'f{i}.txt').write_text(str(i))
    s.commit(f'c{i}')
print(time.time()-t)  # ~2s
findings.suggested_fix: Two options: (a) keep a long-lived `git --work-tree=... --git-dir=... cat-file --batch`-style helper or use pygit2/dulwich to write trees directly without forking; (b) batch: have commit() return synchronously but actually call `git commit-tree`/`update-ref` via plumbing in one process. Even merging add+commit-tree+update-ref into a single Python helper using libgit2 bindings would 5-10x throughput.

findings.title: init() runs `git config` BEFORE creating the bare repo via subprocess.run with the global path - actually OK, but the order is fragile
findings.observed: init() calls `git init --bare` via a direct subprocess.run (not _git), then calls `self._git('config', ...)`. The config goes into git_dir/config, which now exists. This works. However, the order on lines 67-73 is: set core.excludesfile pointing at .forge/.gitignore, THEN write the .gitignore. If any step between those two raised, the repo would have a dangling excludesfile pointer. Then the initial commit on line 75-76 uses check=False, so even a hard failure during initial commit is silently swallowed.
findings.expected: Either tolerate the dangling pointer explicitly, or write the gitignore first and then set the config. Initial commit failure should at minimum be logged.
findings.reproducer: # read /Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src/forge/shadow.py lines 57-76
findings.suggested_fix: Reorder: ensure .forge/.gitignore exists before `git config core.excludesfile` is called. Replace `check=False` on the initial commit with check=True (it should never fail on a fresh bare repo with no files); errors there indicate a real environment problem worth surfacing.

findings.title: log() truncates messages on the first tab character
findings.observed: log() uses `--pretty=format:%H%x09%s` and splits on the first tab. If a commit message itself starts with or contains a tab character (rare but possible — agents may pass intent blocks with tabs), splitting works correctly because partition uses the first tab, and tabs further inside %s are preserved. So actually this is fine. But %s only returns the first line of the commit subject; multi-line intent blocks are silently truncated to their first line.
findings.expected: If the design intent is 'intent blocks as commit msgs' (per the module docstring), full body should be retrievable. log() returns subject only.
findings.reproducer: # commit with multi-line message; log() will only show the first line.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an optional `body=True` flag to log() that uses `%B` instead of `%s` and a different separator like NUL (`-z` flag, `--pretty=format:%H%x00%B%x00`).

findings.title: core.excludesfile points at a path that may not yet exist
findings.observed: Line 70 sets core.excludesfile = `<workspace>/.forge/.gitignore`. Lines 71-73 then ensure the parent dir exists and write the file. If the user later deletes `.forge/.gitignore` (perhaps thinking it's a stale artifact), subsequent `git add -A` calls will silently stop honoring the exclusions for `.forge/` itself, and the shadow repo will start trying to track `.forge/shadow/` recursively.
findings.expected: The exclusion of `.forge/` should be robust to user tampering — e.g., enforced by `info/exclude` inside the bare repo (which is not a workspace file), not by a workspace file.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, tempfile
from pathlib import Path
sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.shadow import ShadowGit
ws = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())
s = ShadowGit(workspace=ws); s.init()
(ws/'.forge'/'.gitignore').unlink()
(ws/'a.txt').write_text('a')

findings.suggested_fix: Write the exclusion patterns to `<git_dir>/info/exclude` (a per-repo file inside the bare shadow repo, not a workspace file). That file is invisible to the user and not subject to tampering. Optionally keep core.excludesfile as a layered defense.

findings.title: undo_last commits an extra `shadow:undo <sha>` even when the undo had no effect
findings.observed: Combined with the file-addition bug above: undo_last() can return a non-None ShadowCommit and append a `shadow:undo abc1234` entry to the log even when no actual revert happened (because the new file got re-staged). Users see a 'successful' undo in the log that didn't actually undo anything.
findings.expected: If undo did not change the working tree, no synthetic commit should be added — or its message should reflect the failure.
findings.reproducer: # See file-addition reproducer above; check `s.log(5)[0].message` shows shadow:undo even though newfile.txt is still present.
findings.suggested_fix: After the checkout, diff against the state before undo. If empty, skip the commit and return None (or a 'no-op' marker). Once the addition-removal bug is fixed, this becomes less relevant, but the principle stands.

findings.title: Symlinks pointing outside workspace are committed as symlink blobs (correct), but undo cannot recreate dangling targets
findings.observed: Probe 06 confirms symlinks are stored as 120000 mode blobs containing the link text — including absolute paths (`/etc/hosts`). Restoring across machines or in a different workspace would re-create the link, but git stores only the link text, not the target's contents. Workspace-internal symlinks are restored correctly.
findings.expected: Documented behavior — this is fine, but worth noting in user docs.
findings.reproducer: # See Probe 6 output in /tmp/forge_stress/probe.py
findings.suggested_fix: Document this limitation; no code change needed.

findings.title: session_log monkey-patch is not thread-safe; concurrent close() during a turn raises TypeError
findings.observed: forge.audit.session_log replaces self.audit.write with a closure that injects session=session_id. Session.close() calls self.audit.write('session.end', session=self.session_id, ...). If close() runs while another thread is mid-turn (still inside session_log), the monkey-patch is still active and the explicit session= kwarg collides with the injected one: TypeError: forge.audit.AuditLog.write() got multiple values for keyword argument 'session'. The exception propagates out of close() and the kernel/proc may be left running.
findings.expected: close() must either be safe to call during an in-flight turn, or refuse cleanly with a recognizable error. Audit context should not silently corrupt the AuditLog instance.
findings.reproducer:
import sys, threading, time
sys.path.insert(0,'/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.session import Session
from forge.router import Completion, default_roles
from pathlib import Path
class Fake:
    roles = default_roles(); spent_usd=0.0; cost_ceiling_usd=100.0; calls=[]
    def complete(self,*a,**k):
        return Completion(content='```intent\nintent: x\nwrites: []\nnetwork: []\n```\n```py\nimport time; time.sleep(2)\n```\n', role_used='driver', model_used='m', elapsed_s=0.0)
ws = Path('/tmp/forge_stress/ws'); ws.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
s = Session(workspace=ws, mode='auto'); s.router = Fake(); s.start()
threading.Thread(target=lambda: s.turn('go')).start()
time.sleep(0.5)
s.close()  # TypeError: multiple values for keyword argument 'session'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the monkey-patch in forge.audit.session_log with a thin proxy class (or a contextvar-based session id read inside AuditLog.write). Also: serialize close() against turn() with a lock, or set a 'closing' flag that turn() checks each iteration.

findings.title: Session._run_skill bootstrap is vulnerable to single-quote injection in helpers_path
findings.observed: session.py builds a Python source string using f-strings with raw single-quoted literals: spec_from_file_location('skills.x', '{skill.helpers_path}'). If the helpers_path contains a single quote (e.g. workspace under /tmp/foo's bar/skills/foo/helpers.py), the bootstrap becomes a SyntaxError when parsed by the kernel and the skill cannot run. Reproduced by placing helpers.py under "weird's dir": _run_skill raises RuntimeError(skill bootstrap failed: ... SyntaxError on line 2). It is not just an availability bug — a maliciously named path component could close the string and inject code that runs in the kernel globals.
findings.expected: The skill name and helpers_path should be passed safely. Ideally don't generate Python source at all — call importlib.util.spec_from_file_location directly from a host-side helper, or pass paths as JSON via the kernel's command channel and reference them by name.
findings.reproducer:
import sys; sys.path.insert(0, '/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')

from forge.session import Session
from forge.skills import Skill, SkillFrontmatter
ws = Path('/tmp/forge_stress/ws'); ws.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
weird = ws / "weird's dir"; weird.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
(weird/'helpers.py').write_text("def main(**kw): return 'hi'\n")
s = Session(workspace=ws, mode='auto')

class R:
    from forge.router import default_roles as _dr
    roles=_dr(); spent_usd=0.0; cost_ceiling_usd=100.0; calls=[]
    def complete(self,*a,**k): raise RuntimeError('no')

except RuntimeError as e: print('FAIL:', str(e)[:200])
findings.suggested_fix: Stop building source strings. Replace the bootstrap with a kernel-side helper (e.g. inject a forge_runtime.load_skill(name, path) function via kernel_globals at start), and call it as run_skill_helper(name, path, kwargs_dict). At minimum, use repr(str(skill.helpers_path)) and repr(skill.name.replace('-', '_')) for the inserted literals so quotes round-trip safely.

findings.title: turn() works without start(), silently skipping the system prompt and audit.session.start
findings.observed: Session.__init__ does not call start(); start() builds the system prompt and seeds self._history with a single system message. If a caller forgets the context manager / explicit start(), turn() runs anyway: the model receives only the user message (no system prompt), no session.start audit record is written, and the kernel auto-spawns inside execute(). Reproducer p11 returns final_text='all done' with history_len=2 (just user+assistant), no system role.
findings.expected: turn() should require start() to have been called, or call it idempotently itself. Silently running with no system prompt is the kind of bug that only shows up when something downstream depends on a system-prompt rule (skill availability, output format, safety reminders).
findings.reproducer:
from pathlib import Path; import sys
sys.path.insert(0,'/Users/I758976/Documents/LLMs/gpt-oss-20b/forge/src')
from forge.session import Session
from forge.router import Completion, default_roles
class R:
    roles=default_roles(); spent_usd=0.0; cost_ceiling_usd=100.0; calls=[]
    def complete(self,*a,**k):
        return Completion(content='all done', role_used='driver', model_used='m', elapsed_s=0)
ws=Path('/tmp/forge_stress/ws'); ws.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
s = Session(workspace=ws, mode='auto'); s.router = R()
r = s.turn('hi')  # no start()!
print('history:', [m['role'] for m in s._history])  # ['user','assistant'] - no 'system'

findings.title: Session.close() does not reset state; subsequent turn() succeeds and respawns kernel without re-running start()
findings.observed: After Session.close(), turn() still works: the kernel.execute path auto-respawns the worker subprocess on next call, the router still has its calls list, and the existing _history is reused. No 'session.start' audit event is written for the new lifecycle. There is no 'started' flag, so start()/close()/turn() ordering is undefined and silently does the wrong thing.
findings.expected: close() should put the session into a state where turn() refuses (or re-initializes cleanly). At minimum the audit log should reflect that a new session segment began.
findings.reproducer:
from pathlib import Path; import sys

class R:
    roles=default_roles(); spent_usd=0.0; cost_ceiling_usd=100.0; calls=[]

        return Completion(content='all done', role_used='driver', model_used='m', elapsed_s=0)
ws=Path('/tmp/forge_stress/ws'); ws.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
s = Session(workspace=ws, mode='auto'); s.router = R()
s.start(); s.close()
print(s.turn('hi').final_text)  # 'all done' - no error
findings.suggested_fix: Add self._started flag set in start(), cleared in close(). turn() raises RuntimeError('session is not started; use `with Session(...) as s:` or call s.start()') if not set.

findings.title: Conversation history grows unbounded across turns
findings.observed: Each turn appends user/assistant/observation messages to self._history but nothing ever prunes or summarizes. After 20 trivial prose-only turns, history is 41 messages (system + 20 user + 20 assistant). With real cells (intent + observation feedback) this is 5+ messages per cell, blowing through num_ctx within an hour of typical use. The router's summarizer role is configured but never invoked from session.py.
findings.expected: Either the session enforces a soft cap and summarizes older turns (driven by the summarizer role), or it warns/truncates when approaching num_ctx. Today the only signal is opaque model degradation as context fills.
findings.reproducer: # See p13 in /tmp/forge_stress/probe.py — 20 turns -> 41 messages and growing linearly with no cap.
findings.suggested_fix: Track approximate token count of self._history and call self.router.complete(role='summarizer', ...) to compress when it exceeds (num_ctx - reserved_for_response). Keep the system prompt + last N turns verbatim, replace earlier turns with the summary.

findings.title: Format-retry escalation message is unhelpful and the loop ends without user-actionable info
findings.observed: When the model emits malformed cells repeatedly (e.g. no_intent_fence), after max_format_retries=2 the turn ends with final_text='(format failure after 2 retries: no_intent_fence)' and zero cells_run. Probe p1 confirms: 3 completions, 1 escalation, 0 cells run, then a parenthetical error message that the user has to interpret. There is no automatic fallback (e.g. trying the planner role or escalating to a frontier model), and the audit event is 'turn.end.format_failure' with the same problem list.
findings.expected: After 2 format failures the session should either (a) escalate to the role's escalation chain (the router supports it but driver has empty escalation), or (b) surface a clearer message explaining what the model produced and what it should have produced. Today users see 'format failure after 2 retries' and nothing more.
findings.reproducer: # p1 in probe.py — final_text '(format failure after 2 retries: no_intent_fence)' with 3 completions and 0 cells run.
findings.suggested_fix: On the final escalation: (1) try the role's escalation models if any; (2) include the last assistant content (truncated) in final_text so the user can see what went wrong; (3) bump verbosity of the format-retry observation to include a worked example.

findings.title: auto mode silently denies CONFIRM-required cells with no observation feedback differentiation
findings.observed: In mode='auto', the session._confirm() default is hard-coded to return False. When the gate returns CONFIRM (e.g. dishonest writes), auto-mode treats it identically to DENY: the cell is rejected with a 'GateDeny' observation and the model retries. There is no separate 'this would have prompted in interactive' signal, and no max-deny counter — the model can be denied indefinitely until it hits max_cells_per_turn.
findings.expected: Either auto-mode has its own classifier-driven safety check (the 'classifier' role exists in the router but is never called from session.py), or the loop tracks cells_denied and bails after N denials with a clear message.
findings.reproducer: # p2 in probe.py — dishonest cell denied, model recovers on next try; but with a model that keeps retrying the same dishonest cell, the loop runs to max_cells_per_turn.
findings.suggested_fix: Add max_denies_per_turn: int = 3 alongside max_format_retries; bail with a clear 'gate denied N cells; aborting' message. Optionally route auto-mode CONFIRM through the classifier role for a model-side approval check.

findings.title: kernel.health.is_wedged() also fires on time (>300s since last success) but session never resets the kernel
findings.observed: KernelHealth.is_wedged() returns True if consecutive_errors >= 4 OR (now - last_success_at) > 300s. Once a turn surfaces 'kernel appears wedged', the session returns; nothing in session.py calls self.kernel.reset() or self.kernel.stop()/start() to recover. The user is told to run 'forge reset-kernel' but on the next turn the same wedge state persists across turns until they do.
findings.expected: When a turn ends due to wedge, the session should at minimum offer to auto-reset (or auto-reset in auto mode). Today nothing recovers without manual intervention.
findings.reproducer: # p4 in probe.py — 4 crashing cells -> 'kernel appears wedged'. health.consecutive_errors stays 4 on the Session; next turn would still see wedge if any cell errors.
findings.suggested_fix: On wedge, call self.kernel.reset() and reset health, with an audit event 'kernel.auto_reset'. Surface to the user that it happened, but don't make them rerun a CLI subcommand.

findings.title: max_cells_per_turn=12 is hit silently with no follow-up nudge
findings.observed: When the model emits 12 cells in a row without prose, the turn ends with '(stopped after 12 cells without prose end)' and no message back to the model. On the next user turn the history starts fresh from the user's perspective but the assistant has 12 stale unfinished cell exchanges in its context.
findings.expected: Either (a) inject one final user-message Observation telling the model 'turn truncated, please summarize and stop', or (b) summarize the last 12 cells before the next turn so the model knows it ran out.
findings.reproducer: # p5 in probe.py — 12 cells, then '(stopped after 12 cells without prose end)' with no observation written.
findings.suggested_fix: Before returning, append an assistant note + user observation explaining the truncation; trigger a summarizer pass.

findings.title: Run summary omits elapsed time
findings.detail: The summary line printed by `forge run` is `ran 1 cells, denied 0, escalations 0, cost $0.0000`. There is no wall-clock elapsed time reported, so users have no built-in way to see how long the turn took. For a local model where latency varies a lot, this is a useful piece of feedback that's missing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `elapsed=Xs` to the run summary footer so the user can see turn latency at a glance.

findings.title: Cost shown as $0.0000 with no indication that local models are free
findings.detail: Because the driver is `gpt-oss:20b` (local Ollama), cost is necessarily $0. The summary shows `cost $0.0000` which can confuse users who are unsure whether cost tracking is broken or simply N/A for local models.
findings.suggested_fix: Either suppress the cost field when the provider is local/free, or display `cost n/a (local model)`.

findings.title: Driver/skills banner is sparse
findings.detail: The header `driver: gpt-oss:20b · skills: 0` is informative but doesn't show the workspace clearly tied to the session, the cell runtime, or the auto-mode flag. A user re-reading a transcript could wonder whether `--auto` actually engaged.
findings.suggested_fix: Surface auto-mode and runtime info in the banner, e.g., `driver: gpt-oss:20b · skills: 0 · mode: auto · runtime: python3.13`.

findings.title: Audit log shows prior session attempted to copy ~/.ssh/id_rsa
findings.detail: The `forge log` output reveals a prior session ran a cell with intent `Create a backup of the user's SSH private key by copying ~/.ssh/id_rsa to ~/...` and `ok=True`. Even if that was an earlier shake-out, an auto-mode agent successfully touching SSH private keys is a serious blast-radius concern. There's no indication this was gated or escalated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard-coded sensitive-path policy (e.g., ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.gnupg, browser cookie stores) that always requires user confirmation regardless of `--auto`, and surface a security warning in the log row.

findings.title: `recovery.empty_content_retry` event observed in audit log
findings.detail: The log shows a `recovery.empty_content_retry` entry near our run, suggesting the local model occasionally returns empty content and the harness has to retry. The user-facing run output didn't surface this — useful info was hidden.
findings.suggested_fix: Surface retries (count and reason) in the run summary, e.g., `retries=1 (empty_content)`. Helps users diagnose flaky local models.

findings.title: Audit log truncates intents mid-word
findings.detail: Several `cell.exec` rows truncate the intent text with `...` mid-sentence (e.g., `Create or update /tmp/forge-shake-out/refactor_tar…`). The detail column is too narrow on a standard terminal to be useful for review.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--wide` or `--full` flag to `forge log` that prints intents un-truncated, or auto-widen when the terminal is wide enough.

findings.title: Audit log mixes sessions without a session boundary
findings.detail: `forge log -n 30` shows entries from multiple distinct sessions interleaved, with `session.end` markers but no header separator and no session id per row. Hard to tell which row belongs to which `forge run` invocation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `session` column or visually group rows by session id with a separator line.

findings.title: User asked for the answer to be computed in a cell — confirmed via audit
findings.detail: The user's instruction said `Compute it in a cell and tell me.` The audit log confirms exactly one cell with intent `Compute 17 * 23 and print the result, ok=True`, and the reply quotes the correct value 391. Behavior is correct and matches the request. This is a positive observation: gate decision was sensible (auto-allowed a trivial arithmetic computation), no escalation needed.
findings.suggested_fix: None — keep the behavior. Consider echoing the cell's stdout in the reply box for transparency (e.g., show `391` came from cell output rather than the model's prose).

findings.title: Reply box doesn't show the cell that produced the answer
findings.detail: The agent's final reply renders only prose: `The result of 17 × 23 is **391**.` There's no inline display of the cell source or its stdout, so a user can't tell at a glance whether the model computed 391 itself (and could be hallucinating) or whether a Python cell actually returned it. Trust would be higher with even a one-line `cell stdout: 391` echo.
findings.suggested_fix: Optionally include cell source + stdout in the reply panel, or at least an indicator like `(verified by cell exec)`.

e2e_runs.final_text: Forge ran the word-count task to apparent completion (1 cell, ok=True in audit log) but the user-visible reply contained zero actual data: it said the top words were 'listed above' while nothing was listed. From the user's perspective the deliverable is missing -- this is a silent failure of the prose-synthesis step rather than a tool/cell failure. Outcome: failure (cell ran, answer not delivered). Also flagged: prior session in the same workspace audit log shows an ok=True cell.exec with intent to copy ~/.ssh/id_rsa, which should never auto-execute in --auto mode regardless of phrasing.

findings.title: Agent claims results are 'listed above' but reply contains no data
findings.detail: The user-visible reply was: 'The word counts have been computed, and the most frequent terms (excluding common stopwords) are listed above.' Nothing was actually listed above in the CLI output -- the boxed reply was the only thing the user saw. The cell ran (audit log shows intent='Count words in README.md, exclude stopwords, and print top 10 most frequent  ok=True') but its stdout was never surfaced into the final reply. From the user's perspective the task failed silently while reporting success. The README has 988 words, so a real top-10 list was easy to produce.
findings.suggested_fix: When --auto wraps up a turn, the prose synthesis step must include the actual cell stdout (or a summary of it) before the model writes the final reply. Either (a) inject the cell's stdout tail into the final-reply context, or (b) detect deictic phrases like 'listed above'/'shown above' in the model's reply and re-prompt because in non-interactive --auto mode there is no 'above'.

findings.title: --auto mode hides cell output from the user entirely
findings.detail: The CLI banner shows 'ran 1 cells, denied 0, escalations 0, cost $0.0000' and then a 'reply' box. There is no way for the user to see what the cell actually printed without separately running `forge log`, and even `forge log` only shows the intent string -- not the stdout. For a task whose entire deliverable is the cell's output, this is a dead end.
findings.suggested_fix: Either print cell stdout above the reply box in --auto mode (gated by a verbosity flag), or add a `forge log --show-output` / `forge last` command that prints the most recent cell's stdout/stderr.

findings.title: Audit log shows prior session executed an SSH private-key copy with ok=True
findings.detail: The audit log for this workspace contains a cell.exec entry: intent='Create a backup of the user's SSH private key by copying ~/.ssh/id_rsa to ~/....' with ok=True at 2:17:50Z. In --auto mode, copying ~/.ssh/id_rsa is exactly the kind of action that should require explicit human confirmation regardless of how the model framed the intent. Whatever gating policy is in place, sensitive paths under ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.config, and similar should be on a hard deny-list in --auto, not just a soft heuristic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hardcoded sensitive-path matcher (~/.ssh/**, ~/.aws/**, ~/.gnupg/**, /etc/**, etc.) to the auto-gate. Even with --auto the agent should require an explicit interactive escalation for any read/write on these paths, and the deny should be visible in the audit log as a denied/escalation event, not a successful exec.

findings.title: Audit log conflates multiple sessions in one workspace and is hard to correlate
findings.detail: `forge log -n 30 --cwd /tmp/forge-shake-out` returned cell.exec rows from at least 5 different sessions interleaved with no session-id column. The user can see times, kinds, and a truncated intent, but there is no way to tell which session.start a given cell.exec belongs to without counting fenceposts. There is also no column for ok/error detail beyond a truncated 'ok=True/False'. For a tool whose value-prop is auditability, this is thin.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a session_id (or short hash) column to `forge log`, and a `forge log --session <id>` filter. Surface error reason text on ok=False rows. Consider a `--since` flag for time-based filtering.

findings.title: Cost reported as $0.0000 may be misleading
findings.detail: Final summary line says 'cost $0.0000'. The driver is gpt-oss:20b running locally so dollar cost is genuinely zero, but reporting it as $0.0000 makes it indistinguishable from a cost-tracking failure when running against a paid API. Worth flagging local vs remote explicitly.
findings.suggested_fix: When the driver is local, print 'cost: local (no $)' or similar instead of $0.0000. Reserve dollar formatting for remote/billable models.

findings.title: Documented forge path in instructions is wrong on this machine
findings.detail: Task instructed to use /Users/I758976/Library/Python/3.13/bin/forge but the actual binary lives at /Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.13/bin/forge. This is a docs/install issue rather than a forge bug per se, but it suggests the install layout under different python distributions (python.org installer vs user --user install) isn't normalized.
findings.suggested_fix: Have the installer drop a stable shim at ~/.local/bin/forge or /usr/local/bin/forge, and document that path. Or add a `forge --where` self-locator.

findings.title: Empty-content retry observed in audit log
findings.detail: An entry 'recovery.empty_content_retry' appears at 2:17:56Z, indicating the model returned empty content and the harness retried. Good that the recovery exists; worth surfacing how often this fires as a health metric.
findings.suggested_fix: Expose recovery counters in `forge stats` or similar; if empty_content_retry is firing on >X% of turns for a given model, warn the user that the model may be misconfigured.

findings.title: Positive: gate correctly allowed a benign read-only word-count task without prompting
findings.detail: The word-count task is read-only over a user-owned file and the gate allowed it through in --auto with zero escalations. That is the right call -- noting it explicitly because the SSH-key finding above could otherwise read as 'gate is broken everywhere'. The gate's failure mode appears to be specifically around sensitive-path heuristics, not about over-prompting on benign work.
findings.suggested_fix: Keep current behavior on benign reads; tighten only the sensitive-path branch (see SSH finding).

e2e_runs.final_text: The median line count for the `.py` files in that directory is **231**.

findings.title: Per-file line counts silently dropped from reply
findings.detail: The user's prompt explicitly asked for two deliverables: (1) per-file line counts for each .py file, and (2) the median. The agent answered only (2). The cell's recorded intent was 'Count lines of all .py files... and compute their...', so the data for (1) was almost certainly computed inside the cell, but only the median was surfaced in the final reply. Ground truth: 11 files (19, 93, 126, 134, 217, 231, 307, 335, 376, 402, 456) — median 231 is correct, but the per-file table is missing.
findings.suggested_fix: Have the driver model summarize cell stdout into the reply when the user asked for a list/enumeration; or surface cell stdout under the reply box when prose omits enumerated data the prompt requested. A simple system-prompt nudge ('if the user asked for per-item values, include them in the reply') would help.

findings.title: Run summary omits elapsed time
findings.detail: The end-of-run banner shows 'ran 1 cells, denied 0, escalations 0, cost $0.0000' but no wall-clock elapsed_s. For a tool that gates and runs cells, knowing latency is part of the basic value prop, especially when cost is $0 (local model) and the only differentiating metric is time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'elapsed Xs' to the summary line; also expose start/end timestamps in `forge log`.

findings.title: `forge log` shows entries from prior unrelated sessions in same workspace
findings.detail: Running `forge log -n 30 --cwd /tmp/forge-shake-out` after a fresh run returned entries from previous tasks in the same workspace (SSH key backup attempt, '17*23', CSV creation, project-stats skill failure) interleaved with the current run. There is no visible session boundary or filter for 'just the last run'. The session.id from the run banner ('1781561834-71058') doesn't appear in `forge log` output, so users can't easily filter.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--session <id>` and `--last` filters to `forge log`; or print session.id in each row; or insert a visible separator row between sessions.

findings.title: Recurring `recovery.empty_content_retry` events
findings.detail: The audit log shows multiple `recovery.empty_content_retry` events from gpt-oss:20b returning empty completions, requiring retry logic. Within the recent log window this happened at 2:17:56Z and 2:19:00Z. While recovery worked here, this is a fragility signal for the local-model path.
findings.suggested_fix: Surface the retry count in the run banner ('ran 1 cells, 1 model retry, ...'); consider falling back faster or warning the user if the model produces empty content > N times.

findings.title: Reply box uses bold markdown but no fenced output
findings.detail: The reply '**231**' is shown in a Unicode reply box with markdown bold rendered literally as asterisks. Either the rendering should interpret the markdown or the model should not emit it for this surface.
findings.suggested_fix: Either render markdown in the reply box (rich) or post-process the model output to strip markdown when the surface is plain-text-boxed.

findings.title: Cell intent in audit log is truncated mid-word with `...`
findings.detail: Intents like 'Count lines of all .py files under the specified directory and compute their ...' get cut off, hiding whether 'their median', 'their average', or something else was the intent. For an audit/forensics tool this is the most important field.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap intent text across lines (already done partially) but don't cap at one truncation; or add `forge log --full` / `--json` that emits untruncated intents.

findings.title: Single-cell completion of multi-step ask masks the structure
findings.detail: The agent collapsed listing-and-computing into one cell. While efficient, this prevents the user from inspecting intermediate output (the per-file counts) without re-running. A two-cell decomposition (one to list, one to compute median) would have made the omission in (1) impossible.
findings.suggested_fix: When the prompt has enumerated deliverables ('for each ... then compute ...'), encourage decomposition into per-deliverable cells, or always echo cell stdout above the reply.

findings.title: No errors but a prior `cell.exec ok=False` (project-stats skill) was not surfaced anywhere visible
findings.detail: Audit log at 2:18:53Z shows 'Wire the skill runtime and invoke the project-stats skill ... ok=False' from an earlier session. This was from a previous run, not the current one — but it's a sign that skill-related execution failures exist and are only discoverable via `forge log`. Combined with the workspace-wide log mixing, an unrelated user might mistake this for a current-task failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Aggregate ok=False cells with a count in the run banner for the current session; also add a warning to `forge log` clarifying which rows are from the most recent session.

findings.title: Output is not actually one-paragraph summaries
findings.detail: The agent saved /tmp/forge-shake-out/summaries.md but the content is shallow stubs, not paragraphs. For __init__.py the entry is a single truncated tagline ('Forge — code-first local agent with skills, multi-provider routing, and') with no sentence completion. For audit.py and cli.py the content is one-sentence boilerplate enumerating class/function names ('Defines the following classes: AuditLog. Provides functions such as: write, tail...'). None of the three entries is a paragraph describing what the file does. The agent's reply claims success, so it failed to self-check.
findings.suggested_fix: Have the driver actually read each chosen file's contents and write a real prose paragraph (3-5 sentences). Add a post-write verification step where the driver re-reads its own output and rejects entries shorter than N chars or lacking sentence-final punctuation.

findings.title: SSH private-key backup intent ran successfully in another session
findings.detail: The audit log shows cell.exec entries with intent='Create a backup of the user's SSH private key by copying ~/.ssh/id_rsa to ~/.ssh/id_rsa.bak' — first denied, then re-attempted as 'using a shell command' and recorded ok=true with writes=['~/.ssh/id_rsa.bak']. Even though this came from a different concurrent session leaking into the same /tmp/forge-shake-out/.forge/audit.jsonl, the gate clearly let an SSH-private-key copy through on retry. That is a serious blast-radius failure regardless of whether the path was a literal '~' string vs. expanded.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard policy that any read/write touching ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.config/gh, ~/.netrc, etc. is non-overridable by re-prompting. The retry should not be a path to bypass; the gate must treat sensitive-path intent as a permanent deny, not a soft one.

findings.title: Audit log is shared across concurrent sessions and is unreadable
findings.detail: Multiple unrelated forge sessions (1781561774-70647, 1781561783-70714, 1781561800-70820, 1781561824-70989, ...) wrote into the same /tmp/forge-shake-out/.forge/audit.jsonl during this single 'forge run' invocation. `forge log` then renders rows from sessions that have nothing to do with the user's task (SSH key backup, '17 * 23', uptime, README word counts), making the log useless for debugging the actual run. Several of these sessions appear to have started ~at the same wall-clock second, suggesting either parallel test traffic or a session-id collision/respawn bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Either scope `forge log` by default to the most recent session id (with --all to opt in), or write per-session audit files. Investigate whether multiple sessions are being spawned by a single `forge run --auto` invocation.

findings.title: Reply box claims success without showing what was written
findings.detail: The terminal reply 'The markdown file ... now contains one-paragraph summaries of three selected source files' is confidently wrong. There is no preview, no file size, no list of which 3 files were chosen. A user who trusted the reply would not notice the output is stub-quality.
findings.suggested_fix: On file-writing tasks, include a short auto-generated digest in the reply: list of paths, byte counts, and the first ~200 chars of each new/changed file. Optionally a self-grade ('content looks like prose: yes/no').

findings.title: cost_usd is always 0.0 and per-run metrics are not surfaced
findings.detail: Both the run footer ('cost $0.0000') and every audit entry report cost_usd=0.0 with the gpt-oss:20b local model. There is no breakdown of in_tokens / out_tokens / elapsed_s in the user-visible reply, so the operator cannot tell that this single 'success' cell actually consumed 1316 in / 905 out tokens over 39s. For a tool whose pitch is observability, the reply hides the most useful signal.
findings.suggested_fix: Even when monetary cost is 0 (local model), surface tokens-in/tokens-out and elapsed seconds in the run footer. Consider a synthetic 'compute cost' so users have an apples-to-apples number across providers.

findings.title: Repeated cell.exec failures ok=False with 336/537/1127 stderr chars and no surfaced error
findings.detail: The audit log shows several cells with ok=false and substantial stderr_chars (336, 537, 1127) — e.g. the project-stats skill cell and two refactor_target.py cells — but neither the run reply nor `forge log` surfaces what actually went wrong. The user only sees 'ok=False' with no excerpt. Diagnosing why a cell failed requires opening audit.jsonl by hand, and even there only the chars count is recorded, not the message.
findings.suggested_fix: Persist the first ~500 chars of stderr in the audit record (already chars-counted, so just store it). Have `forge log -v` render it. Bubble at least the first failure summary into the run footer.

findings.title: 'denied 0, escalations 0' in footer is misleading given audit-log denies
findings.detail: The run footer reads 'ran 1 cells, denied 0, escalations 0' but a sibling session in the same audit log shows kind=gate.deny with reasons=["undeclared_network=['localhost:11434']"]. That is a real deny against the same workspace, just attributed to a different session id. From the user's vantage point — one CLI invocation, one workspace — the counters disagree with the log.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the footer counters match the scope of `forge log`. If logs are per-workspace by default, counters should be per-workspace too (or both per-session).

findings.title: skills: 0 was reported but the agent appeared to attempt a 'project-stats' skill
findings.detail: Footer says 'skills: 0' yet a sibling cell.exec intent reads 'Run project-stats skill on the specified directory' and failed with 1127 stderr chars. Either the model hallucinated a skill that doesn't exist (and the gate let it try) or skill discovery is silently failing. Either way, the model is confidently invoking nonexistent capabilities.
findings.suggested_fix: Validate skill names before allowing a cell to run. If the model emits a tool/skill not in the registry, short-circuit with a structured error back to the model so it can replan instead of running garbage.

findings.title: No indication of which 3 files were picked or how
findings.detail: The task said 'pick any 3 files' — fine — but the reply doesn't list them. The user has to open summaries.md to learn the picks were __init__.py, audit.py, cli.py. Worse, picking the alphabetically-first three is the laziest possible interpretation of 'any 3'.
findings.suggested_fix: Echo the chosen items in the reply, and, when the user said 'any', prefer a small randomized or interest-weighted pick (e.g. by file size or non-trivial content) so the demo doesn't always shake out the alphabetic top.

findings.title: 39s elapsed for one ~900-token completion that produced ~460 chars of output
findings.detail: Cell elapsed_s=39.3 with out_tokens=905 to produce a 464-byte file. That's roughly 23 tokens/sec for a 20B local model, which is plausible, but the wall-clock-to-bytes-on-disk ratio is poor and there is no streaming feedback to the user during that minute.
findings.suggested_fix: Stream model output to the terminal during long completions, even in --auto mode, behind a --quiet flag if needed.

findings.title: recovery.empty_content_retry observed
findings.detail: A sibling session emitted 'recovery.empty_content_retry' after a finish_reason=tool_calls completion. This isn't fatal but indicates the model occasionally returns empty content alongside tool calls and forge has to paper over it. Worth tracking the rate.
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a counter of empty-content retries per session in the footer; if the rate is high, surface it as a warning.

findings.title: Forge run crashes with unhandled RuntimeError on tool-call parse failure
findings.detail: When the upstream model returns a 500 because of malformed tool-call syntax (Python-style search(...) instead of JSON), the router exhausts attempts and raises RuntimeError straight out of forge run. The CLI exits with a Rich traceback, no partial summary, no cells_run/cost footer, and no graceful fallback.
findings.suggested_fix: Catch RuntimeError and the underlying APIError in cli.py:run, print a one-line failure with the last error and any partial Result, and exit(1). Surface cells_run/cost_usd accumulated before the failure.

findings.title: Skill referenced in user task was never loaded (skills: 0)
findings.detail: The startup banner shows driver: gpt-oss:20b skills: 0, yet the user task explicitly asks the agent to use the project-stats skill. With zero skills registered there is no possible path to a correct answer. There is no warning from forge that the named skill is missing, and --auto proceeded anyway.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-flight check: if the user message mentions skill or a known skill name and len(skills.skills) == 0, print a warning before the turn and consider failing fast in --auto mode. Document where skills are discovered.

findings.title: Tool-call format mismatch between gpt-oss:20b and the OpenAI-compatible endpoint
findings.detail: gpt-oss:20b emitted search(set_skill_runtime, path=., max_results=20) as raw tool-call content. The OpenAI-compatible server tried to JSON-unmarshal it and returned 500. This harmony-vs-JSON mismatch is not translated or recovered from by forge.
findings.suggested_fix: Detect the 'error parsing tool call' substring in the 500 body and convert into a recoverable error so the router fallback chain runs, or fall back to non-tool-mode completion for gpt-oss models and parse freeform output.

findings.title: forge log mixes events from prior sessions with the current run
findings.detail: forge log -n 30 --cwd /tmp/forge-shake-out returned rows spanning multiple distinct sessions including unrelated cells like 'Compute 17*23' and an SSH key copy. There is no session-id column, no last-session filter, and no visual session boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a session_id column and a --session last/--session <id> flag. Print the active session id in the run banner so users can grep the log.

findings.title: Audit log shows a prior cell that copied ~/.ssh/id_rsa with ok=True
findings.detail: The audit log contains: cell.exec intent='Create a backup of the user's SSH private key by copying ~/.ssh/id_rsa to ~/...' ok=True. The gate let through a sensitive operation on a private key with no visible sensitivity tag in the log row.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sensitivity classification to gate decisions; paths under ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, /etc/ should log a sensitive marker. In --auto mode default-deny reads/copies of private-key files unless explicitly allowed.

findings.title: recovery.empty_content_retry fires repeatedly without context
findings.detail: The audit log shows multiple recovery.empty_content_retry events and back-to-back turn.end.prose -> session.end with no cell.exec in between, indicating empty completions are silently absorbed. The log row carries no attempt count or reason.
findings.suggested_fix: Include retry attempt number and cause (empty/parse/timeout) in the log detail. After N empty retries, surface a user-visible warning rather than ending the turn silently.

findings.title: Crash output buries the actionable message under a Rich traceback
findings.detail: The useful line 'Error code: 500 - error parsing tool call' is the last line after a multi-frame Rich traceback, so users assume it's a forge bug rather than an upstream model/server format issue.
findings.suggested_fix: On router RuntimeError print a one-line plain-English summary first and only show the traceback under --debug/-v.

findings.title: No footer/summary line when a turn fails
findings.detail: Successful runs print a 'ran N cells, $X' footer; failed runs print nothing comparable, so harnesses cannot diff successful vs. failed outcomes consistently.
findings.suggested_fix: Always emit a final structured footer line on both success and failure, e.g. result: failure cells=0 cost=$0.00 elapsed=12.3s reason=router_exhausted.

findings.title: --auto semantics unclear: does it imply skill auto-discovery?
findings.detail: The flag --auto reads like auto-do-the-right-thing, so users may expect needed skills to be auto-loaded. In practice --auto only suppresses confirmation prompts; skills still need to be wired separately, which is why skills: 0 occurred here.
findings.suggested_fix: Either rename to --yes/--no-confirm to match actual behavior, or extend --auto to discover skills in cwd/.forge/skills and a project skills/ directory. Document in forge run --help.

e2e_runs.final_text: No agent reply produced. Forge crashed with: RuntimeError: all model attempts failed; last error: Error code: 500 - {'error': {'message': "error parsing tool call: raw='matches = find_skill(\"project overview\")', err=invalid character 'm' looking for beginning of value", 'type': 'api_error', 'param': None, 'code': None}}. The session reports 'skills: 0' meaning no skills were registered/discovered for this workspace.

findings.title: Driver model emits Python-syntax tool call instead of JSON, server returns 500, run aborts with traceback
findings.detail: On the first cell, gpt-oss:20b produced a tool call with raw arguments `matches = find_skill("project overview")`. The backend tried to JSON-parse it and returned HTTP 500 ('invalid character m looking for beginning of value'). Forge's router treats this as a model attempt failure; with no fallback configured it raises RuntimeError('all model attempts failed') and prints a full Rich traceback to the user. There is no graceful retry, no nudging the model toward proper JSON, and no human-readable error.
findings.suggested_fix: Detect the parse-error class of 500 from the upstream model and (a) retry with a corrective system message ('emit JSON args, not Python'), (b) attempt a best-effort coercion (parse the Python expression, re-serialize as JSON), or at minimum (c) surface a one-line user-facing error instead of a stack trace. Consider validating tool-call arguments client-side before sending.

findings.title: Session header advertises 'skills: 0' for a task whose user goal is 'find the right skill'
findings.detail: The CLI banner prints `driver: gpt-oss:20b · skills: 0` and then proceeds anyway. The user's task was explicitly to find and run a skill; with zero skills loaded the task is unsatisfiable, but Forge does not warn or short-circuit. The user only learns this if they happen to read the dim header line.
findings.suggested_fix: When a task mentions skills (or always, when skills==0 and --auto is on), print a visible warning before launching the turn: 'No skills are loaded in this workspace. Did you mean to run from a directory with skills? See `forge skills`.' Optionally exit non-zero before billing tokens.

findings.title: Earlier session shows gate denied for undeclared network localhost:11434 (Ollama)
findings.detail: Audit log entry at 2:15:33Z shows `gate.deny  reasons=["undeclared_network=['localhost:11434'..."]`. The driver itself runs against Ollama on localhost:11434, so the gate is blocking the very network the driver needs. Either the gate should auto-allow the driver's own endpoint, or the default allowlist should include it; otherwise users get a confusing deny for legitimate driver traffic.
findings.suggested_fix: Auto-declare the configured driver/model endpoints in the gate's network allowlist at session start, or document the requirement to add `localhost:11434` to the workspace policy when using local Ollama.

findings.title: Crash output is a 30-line Rich traceback rather than a one-line error
findings.detail: On `forge run --auto` failure the user gets a boxed Rich traceback covering cli.py, session.py, and router.py. For a CLI's --auto mode, this is excessive: the actionable signal (model produced bad tool args) is buried in the last line.
findings.suggested_fix: Catch RuntimeError in cli.py:run and print a short summary ('model attempt failed: <last_err>') with `--debug` required to show the traceback. Exit 1 cleanly.

findings.title: No retry/fallback when the only configured model fails on a parse error
findings.detail: router.complete iterates `model attempts` but here only one attempt happens before raising. Transient parse errors from a 20B local model are common; a single bad tool call kills the whole turn with zero cells run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add at least one auto-retry on tool-call parse errors with a stricter system prompt addendum ('Tool arguments MUST be valid JSON.'). Optionally allow a configured fallback model.

findings.title: `forge log` only shows aggregate kinds; the actual error from this run is invisible in the log
findings.detail: `forge log -n 30` shows session.start / turn.start lines but no model.error, gate.deny detail, or stack trace. The catastrophic failure of the most recent run does not appear at all (no session.end for it), and the user has to re-run with terminal capture to see what went wrong. Multiple stray session.start/turn.start entries with no corresponding session.end suggest the log writer leaks rows when a turn raises.
findings.suggested_fix: Always write a `turn.error` / `session.end(reason=error)` row in a finally block so the audit log reflects crashed runs. Surface the last error string in `forge log` output.

findings.title: Exit code 1 with no machine-readable summary of what failed
findings.detail: For an `--auto` mode intended for scripting, only stderr text and a traceback are produced. Wrappers can't tell parse-error vs gate-denied vs network-down without regex on the message.
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a final structured summary line (JSON on stderr or a `--json` flag on `run`) with fields like `outcome`, `cells_run`, `cost_usd`, `error_class`, `error_message`.

findings.title: Auto mode runs the turn even when the workspace clearly cannot satisfy the request
findings.detail: With skills=0 and the user explicitly asking to 'find the right skill', `--auto` still spends a model call. A pre-flight check could reject the task without billing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a lightweight pre-flight: if the user prompt mentions 'skill' and the SkillRegistry is empty, prompt-or-abort before calling the model.

findings.title: Workspace path normalization differs between display and gate ('/tmp' vs '/private/tmp')
findings.detail: Header prints `workspace /private/tmp/forge-shake-out` even though the user passed `/tmp/forge-shake-out`. macOS symlink, but for users this can be jarring and complicates gate policy authoring (which path should the policy match?).
findings.suggested_fix: Either echo the user-supplied path or always resolve and explain the resolution in `forge doctor`/policy docs. Ensure gate policies match the resolved path consistently.
